title: Mobile, Alabama

infobox.value: [Pierre Le Moyne](/wiki/Pierre_Le_Moyne_d%27Iberville "Pierre Le Moyne d'Iberville")  [Jean-Baptiste Le Moyne](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Le_Moyne_de_Bienville "Jean-Baptiste Le Moyne de Bienville")
US: [141st](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") AL: [4th](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Alabama "List of municipalities in Alabama")

([/moʊˈbiːl/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*moh-BEEL*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), French: [[mɔbil]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Fr-mobile.ogg "File:Fr-mobile.ogg")) is a city and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Mobile County, Alabama](/wiki/Mobile_County,_Alabama "Mobile County, Alabama"), United States. The population was 187,041 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census")[[5]](#cite_note-2020_Census_(City)-6) and estimated at 204,689 following an annexation in 2023, making it the [second-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Alabama "List of municipalities in Alabama") in Alabama. The [Mobile metropolitan area](/wiki/Mobile_metropolitan_area "Mobile metropolitan area"), with an estimated 412,000 people, is the [third-most populous metropolitan area](/wiki/Alabama_statistical_areas "Alabama statistical areas") in the state.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

**Mobile** ([/moʊˈbiːl/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*moh-BEEL*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), French: [[mɔbil]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Fr-mobile.ogg "File:Fr-mobile.ogg")) is a city and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Mobile County, Alabama](/wiki/Mobile_County,_Alabama "Mobile County, Alabama"), United States. The population was 187,041 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census")[[5]](#cite_note-2020_Census_(City)-6) and estimated at 204,689 following an annexation in 2023, making it the [second-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Alabama "List of municipalities in Alabama") in Alabama. The [Mobile metropolitan area](/wiki/Mobile_metropolitan_area "Mobile metropolitan area"), with an estimated 412,000 people, is the [third-most populous metropolitan area](/wiki/Alabama_statistical_areas "Alabama statistical areas") in the state.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

Alabama's only deep-water port, Mobile is located on the [Mobile River](/wiki/Mobile_River "Mobile River") at the head of [Mobile Bay](/wiki/Mobile_Bay "Mobile Bay") on the north-central [Gulf Coast](/wiki/Gulf_Coast_of_the_United_States "Gulf Coast of the United States").[[11]](#cite_note-foundmob-12) The [Port of Mobile](/wiki/Port_of_Mobile "Port of Mobile") has always played a key role in the economic health of the city, beginning with the settlement as an important trading center between the [French colonists](/wiki/French_colonization_of_the_Americas "French colonization of the Americas") and [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), and now to its current role as the 12th-largest port in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[12]](#cite_note-mobilianpid-13)[[13]](#cite_note-port-14) During the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), the city surrendered to Federal forces on April 12, 1865,[[14]](#cite_note-15) after [Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)") victories at two forts protecting the city.

Considered one of the [Gulf Coast](/wiki/Gulf_Coast_of_the_United_States "Gulf Coast of the United States")'s cultural centers, Mobile has several art museums, a symphony orchestra, professional opera, professional ballet company, and a large concentration of historic architecture.[[15]](#cite_note-mma2-16)[[16]](#cite_note-srg-17) Mobile is known for having [Mardi Gras](/wiki/Mardi_Gras "Mardi Gras"), the oldest organized [Carnival](/wiki/Carnival "Carnival") celebration in the United States. [Alabama's French Creole population](/wiki/Alabama_Creole_people "Alabama Creole people") celebrated this festival from the first decade of the 18th century. Beginning in 1830, Mobile was home to the first organized Carnival [mystic society](/wiki/Mystic_society "Mystic society") to celebrate with a parade in the United States.[[17]](#cite_note-MMtime-18)

## History

Main article: [History of Mobile, Alabama](/wiki/History_of_Mobile,_Alabama "History of Mobile, Alabama")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Mobile, Alabama](/wiki/Timeline_of_Mobile,_Alabama "Timeline of Mobile, Alabama").

### Etymology

Further information: [Alabama Creole people](/wiki/Alabama_Creole_people "Alabama Creole people")

The city is named after the Mobile tribe that the French colonists encountered living around [Mobile Bay](/wiki/Mobile_Bay "Mobile Bay").[[18]](#cite_note-19) Although it is debated by Alabama historians, they may have been descendants of the Native American tribe from the small fortress town, [Mabila](/wiki/Mabila "Mabila"), in central Alabama.[[19]](#cite_note-maubilianind-20) The Mobile tribe became allies with the French colonists and suggested the location for the original town of Mobile and a river fort.[[20]](#cite_note-21) The tribe's language was the basis for [Mobilian Jargon](/wiki/Mobilian_Jargon "Mobilian Jargon"), a Choctaw-derived *lingua franca* widely used to facilitate trade among the various Gulf Coast peoples.[[21]](#cite_note-Waselkov-2005-22) About seven years after the founding of the French Mobile settlement, the Mobile tribe, along with the Tohomé, gained permission from the colonists to settle near the fort.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

### Colonial

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Fort_Cond%C3%A9_2.jpg/250px-Fort_Cond%C3%A9_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fort_Cond%C3%A9_2.jpg)

A reconstructed bastion of the Fort Condé

In 1702, French colonists founded the [Old Mobile Site](/wiki/Old_Mobile_Site "Old Mobile Site") south of existing Native American villages on the Mobile River. The *Fort Louis de la Louisiane* was built on a bluff that today is 27 miles (43 km) upriver from the [Mobile River](/wiki/Mobile_River "Mobile River")'s mouth. The original town of Mobile was built on lower ground just downriver from the fort.[[21]](#cite_note-Waselkov-2005-22) [French Canadian](/wiki/French_Canadian "French Canadian") brothers [Pierre Le Moyne d'Iberville](/wiki/Pierre_Le_Moyne_d%27Iberville "Pierre Le Moyne d'Iberville") and [Jean-Baptiste Le Moyne, Sieur de Bienville](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Le_Moyne,_Sieur_de_Bienville "Jean-Baptiste Le Moyne, Sieur de Bienville") founded the site to establish control over France's claims to *La Louisiane*. From 1702 to 1711, it was the French colonial capital.[[23]](#cite_note-nhlapp-24) Mobile's Roman Catholic parish was established on July 20, 1703, by [Jean-Baptiste de la Croix de Chevrières de Saint-Vallier](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_de_la_Croix_de_Chevri%C3%A8res_de_Saint-Vallier "Jean-Baptiste de la Croix de Chevrières de Saint-Vallier"), [Bishop of Quebec](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Quebec "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Quebec"), and was the first French Catholic parish established on the [Gulf Coast](/wiki/Gulf_Coast_of_the_United_States "Gulf Coast of the United States").[[24]](#cite_note-oldmobile1-25) In 1704, the ship [*Pélican*](/wiki/French_ship_P%C3%A9lican_(1702) "French ship Pélican (1702)") delivered 23 Frenchwomen to the colony, but passengers had contracted [yellow fever](/wiki/Yellow_fever "Yellow fever") at a stop in [Havana](/wiki/Havana "Havana"); though most recovered, many colonists and neighboring Native Americans contracted the disease and died. African [slaves](/wiki/Slaves "Slaves") were transported to Mobile on a supply ship from the French colony of [Saint-Domingue](/wiki/Saint-Domingue "Saint-Domingue") in the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean").[[25]](#cite_note-pelican-26)

Disease and flooding plagued French colonists at the Old Mobile Site.[[26]](#cite_note-Thomason-2001-27) The colony grew to 279 persons by 1708 but shrank to 178 persons two years later.[[24]](#cite_note-oldmobile1-25) Bienville ordered the settlement to relocate downriver and Mobile moved to its present location at the confluence of the [Mobile River](/wiki/Mobile_River "Mobile River") and [Mobile Bay](/wiki/Mobile_Bay "Mobile Bay") in 1711.[[26]](#cite_note-Thomason-2001-27) According to anthropologist Greg Waselkov, French colonists burned the Old Mobile Site to the ground, likely to prevent their enemies from occupying it.[[27]](#cite_note-hoffman-28) An earth-and-palisade Fort Louis was constructed at the new site.[[28]](#cite_note-MoMfort-29) The capital of [La Louisiane](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_France) "Louisiana (New France)") was moved in 1720 to [Biloxi](/wiki/Biloxi,_Mississippi "Biloxi, Mississippi"),[[28]](#cite_note-MoMfort-29) and Mobile became a regional military and trading center. In 1723 the construction of a new brick fort with a stone foundation began[[28]](#cite_note-MoMfort-29) and it was renamed Fort Condé in honor of [Louis Henri, Duke of Bourbon](/wiki/Louis_Henri,_Duke_of_Bourbon "Louis Henri, Duke of Bourbon").[[29]](#cite_note-conde1-30)

In 1763, Britain defeated France in the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"). The [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)") ceded French territories east of the Mississippi River to Britain, including Mobile. The city became part of the expanded British [West Florida](/wiki/West_Florida "West Florida") colony.[[30]](#cite_note-setmob1-31) The British changed the name of Fort Condé to [Fort Charlotte](/wiki/Fort_Charlotte,_Mobile "Fort Charlotte, Mobile"), after [Queen Charlotte](/wiki/Queen_Charlotte "Queen Charlotte").[[31]](#cite_note-setmob2-32)

The British promised religious tolerance to the French colonists, and 112 French colonists remained in Mobile.[[32]](#cite_note-britmob1-33) The first permanent Jewish settlers came to Mobile in 1763 as a result of the new British rule and religious tolerance; Jews were not allowed to officially reside in colonial French Louisiana due to the [Code Noir](/wiki/Code_Noir "Code Noir"). Most colonial-era Jews in Mobile were merchants and traders from Sephardic Jewish communities in [Savannah, Georgia](/wiki/Savannah,_Georgia "Savannah, Georgia") and [Charleston, South Carolina](/wiki/Charleston,_South_Carolina "Charleston, South Carolina").[[33]](#cite_note-34) By 1766, the town's population was estimated to be 860 people, although the borders were smaller than during the French colonial period.[[32]](#cite_note-britmob1-33) During the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), West Florida and Mobile became a refuge for [loyalists](/wiki/Loyalist_(American_Revolution) "Loyalist (American Revolution)") fleeing the other colonies.[[34]](#cite_note-storymobile1-35)

While the British were fighting rebellious colonists along the Atlantic coast, the [Spanish entered the war](/wiki/Spain_in_the_American_Revolutionary_War "Spain in the American Revolutionary War") in 1779.[[35]](#cite_note-barrancas-36) The Spanish wished to eliminate any British threat to their Louisiana colony west of the Mississippi River, which they had received from France in the 1763 Treaty of Paris.[[34]](#cite_note-storymobile1-35) [Bernardo de Galvez](/wiki/Bernardo_de_G%C3%A1lvez_y_Madrid,_Count_of_G%C3%A1lvez "Bernardo de Gálvez y Madrid, Count of Gálvez"), the Spanish Governor of Louisiana,[[35]](#cite_note-barrancas-36) captured Mobile during the [Battle of Fort Charlotte](/wiki/Battle_of_Fort_Charlotte "Battle of Fort Charlotte") in 1780. Their actions were condoned by the revolting American colonies, partially evidenced by the presence of Oliver Pollack, representative of the American Continental Congress. Due to strong trade ties, many residents of Mobile and [West Florida](/wiki/West_Florida "West Florida") remained loyal to the [British Crown](/wiki/British_Crown "British Crown").[[34]](#cite_note-storymobile1-35)[[35]](#cite_note-barrancas-36) The Spanish renamed the fort as Fortaleza Carlota, and held Mobile as a part of Spanish [West Florida](/wiki/West_Florida "West Florida") until 1813, when it was seized by United States General [James Wilkinson](/wiki/James_Wilkinson "James Wilkinson") during the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812").[[36]](#cite_note-wilkinson-37)

### 19th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Southern_Hotel_Water_Street.jpg/250px-Southern_Hotel_Water_Street.jpg)](/wiki/File:Southern_Hotel_Water_Street.jpg)

A [HABS](/wiki/Historic_American_Buildings_Survey "Historic American Buildings Survey") photo of the Southern Hotel on Water Street in 1934. It was completed in 1837 and demolished soon after this photograph was taken.

When Mobile was included in the [Mississippi Territory](/wiki/Mississippi_Territory "Mississippi Territory") in 1813, the population had dwindled to roughly 300 people. The territory was split in 1817, and the eastern half, including the Mobile Bay area, became the [Alabama Territory](/wiki/Alabama_Territory "Alabama Territory") for two years before being admitted to the union as the state of Alabama. Mobile's population had increased to 809 by that time.[[37]](#cite_note-antebellum1-38) Mobile was well situated for trade, as its location tied it to a river system that served as the principal navigational access for most of Alabama and a large part of Mississippi. River transportation was aided by the introduction of [steamboats](/wiki/Steamboat "Steamboat") in the early decades of the 19th century.[[38]](#cite_note-39) By 1822, the city's population had risen to 2,800.[[37]](#cite_note-antebellum1-38)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Convent_Visitation_01.jpg/250px-Convent_Visitation_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Convent_Visitation_01.jpg)

[Convent and Academy of the Visitation](/wiki/Convent_and_Academy_of_the_Visitation "Convent and Academy of the Visitation"), completed in 1855

The [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") in Great Britain created shortages of cotton, increasing prices on world markets.[[39]](#cite_note-40) Much land well suited to growing cotton lies in the vicinity of the [Mobile River](/wiki/Mobile_River "Mobile River"), and its main tributaries the [Tombigbee](/wiki/Tombigbee_River "Tombigbee River") and [Alabama Rivers](/wiki/Alabama_River "Alabama River"). A [plantation economy](/wiki/Plantation_economy "Plantation economy") using slave labor developed in the region and Mobile's population quickly grew.[[37]](#cite_note-antebellum1-38) From the 1830s onward, Mobile expanded into a city of commerce focused on the cotton and slave trades. Slaves were transported by ship in the [coastwise slave trade](/wiki/Coastwise_slave_trade "Coastwise slave trade") from the Upper South. Many businesses in the city were related to the slave trade, and the city's booming businesses attracted merchants from the North; by 1850 10% of its population was from [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), which was deeply involved in the cotton industry.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

Mobile was the slave-trading center of the state until the 1850s, when it was surpassed by [Montgomery](/wiki/Montgomery,_Alabama "Montgomery, Alabama").[[41]](#cite_note-antebellum3-42) The prosperity stimulated a building boom that was underway by the mid-1830s. This was cut short in part by the [Panic of 1837](/wiki/Panic_of_1837 "Panic of 1837") and [yellow fever](/wiki/Yellow_fever "Yellow fever") epidemics.[[42]](#cite_note-antebellum2-43) The waterfront was developed with wharves, terminal facilities, and fireproof brick warehouses. The exports of cotton grew in proportion to the amounts being produced in the [Black Belt](/wiki/Black_Belt_(region_of_Alabama) "Black Belt (region of Alabama)"); by 1840 Mobile was second only to [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") in cotton exports in the nation.[[37]](#cite_note-antebellum1-38) Mobile slaveholders owned relatively few slaves compared to planters in the upland [plantation](/wiki/Plantations_in_the_American_South "Plantations in the American South") areas, but many households had domestic slaves, and many other slaves worked on the waterfront and on riverboats. The last slaves to enter the United States from the African trade were brought to Mobile on the slave ship [*Clotilda*](/wiki/Clotilda_(slave_ship) "Clotilda (slave ship)"), including [Cudjoe Lewis](/wiki/Cudjoe_Lewis "Cudjoe Lewis"), who was the last survivor of the slave trade.[[43]](#cite_note-Hurston-1927-44)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Steamer_loading_cotton_in_Mobile.jpg/250px-Steamer_loading_cotton_in_Mobile.jpg)](/wiki/File:Steamer_loading_cotton_in_Mobile.jpg)

[Steamboats](/wiki/Steamboat "Steamboat") bound for inland Alabama and Mississippi being loaded at Mobile's dockyards

By 1860 Mobile's population within the city limits had reached 29,258 people; it was the 27th-largest city in the United States and 4th-largest in what would soon be the [Confederate States of America](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America").[[44]](#cite_note-1860cen-45) The free population in the whole of Mobile County, including the city, consisted of 29,754 citizens, of which 1,195 were [free people of color](/wiki/Person_of_color "Person of color").[[45]](#cite_note-pop1860-46) Additionally, 1,785 slave owners in the county held 11,376 people in bondage, about one-quarter of the total county population of 41,130 people.[[45]](#cite_note-pop1860-46)

During the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), Mobile was a Confederate city. The *[H. L. Hunley](/wiki/H._L._Hunley_(submarine) "H. L. Hunley (submarine)")*, the first [submarine](/wiki/Submarine "Submarine") to sink an enemy ship, was built in Mobile.[[46]](#cite_note-Hunley-47) One of the most famous [naval engagements](/wiki/Naval_engagement "Naval engagement") of the [war](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") was the [Battle of Mobile Bay](/wiki/Battle_of_Mobile_Bay "Battle of Mobile Bay"), resulting in the [Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)") taking control of [Mobile Bay](/wiki/Mobile_Bay "Mobile Bay") on August 5, 1864.[[47]](#cite_note-newhist1-48) On April 12, 1865, three days after [Robert E. Lee](/wiki/Robert_E._Lee "Robert E. Lee")'s surrender at [Appomattox Courthouse](/wiki/Battle_of_Appomattox_Courthouse "Battle of Appomattox Courthouse"), the city surrendered to the [Union army](/wiki/Union_army "Union army") to avoid destruction after Union victories at nearby [Spanish Fort](/wiki/Battle_of_Spanish_Fort "Battle of Spanish Fort") and [Fort Blakeley](/wiki/Battle_of_Fort_Blakeley "Battle of Fort Blakeley").[[47]](#cite_note-newhist1-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Mobile_Cotton_Exchange.jpg/250px-Mobile_Cotton_Exchange.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mobile_Cotton_Exchange.jpg)

[Mobile Cotton Exchange](/wiki/Mobile_Cotton_Exchange "Mobile Cotton Exchange") and Chamber of Commerce building, completed in 1886

On May 25, 1865, the city suffered great loss when some three hundred people died as a result of an [explosion](/wiki/Mobile_magazine_explosion "Mobile magazine explosion") at a [federal](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") [ammunition depot](/wiki/Ammunition_depot "Ammunition depot") on Beauregard Street. The explosion left a 30-foot (9 m) deep hole at the depot's location, and sank ships docked on the Mobile River; the resulting fires destroyed the northern portion of the city.[[48]](#cite_note-49)

Federal [Reconstruction](/wiki/Reconstruction_era_of_the_United_States "Reconstruction era of the United States") in Mobile began after the Civil War and effectively ended in 1874 when the local [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") gained control of the city government.[[49]](#cite_note-reconstruction1-50) The last quarter of the 19th century was a time of economic depression and municipal insolvency for Mobile. One example can be provided by the value of Mobile's exports during this period of depression. The value of exports leaving the city fell from $9 million in 1878 to $3 million in 1882.[[50]](#cite_note-exports1878-51)

### 20th century before WWII

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Van_Antwerp_Building_1907.jpg/250px-Van_Antwerp_Building_1907.jpg)](/wiki/File:Van_Antwerp_Building_1907.jpg)

The [Van Antwerp Building](/wiki/Van_Antwerp_Building "Van Antwerp Building"), completed in 1907

The turn of the 20th century brought the [Progressive Era](/wiki/Progressive_Era "Progressive Era") to Mobile. The economic structure developed with new industries, generating new jobs and attracting a significant increase in population.[[51]](#cite_note-progress1-52) The population increased from around 40,000 in 1900 to 60,000 by 1920.[[51]](#cite_note-progress1-52) During this time the city received $3 million in federal grants for harbor improvements to deepen the shipping channels.[[51]](#cite_note-progress1-52) During and after World War I, manufacturing became increasingly vital to Mobile's economic health, with shipbuilding and steel production being two of the most important industries.[[51]](#cite_note-progress1-52)

During this time, social justice and race relations in Mobile worsened.[[51]](#cite_note-progress1-52) The state passed a new constitution in 1901 that [disenfranchised most blacks and many poor whites](/wiki/Disfranchisement_after_Reconstruction_era "Disfranchisement after Reconstruction era"); and the white Democratic-dominated legislature passed other discriminatory legislation. In 1902, the city government passed Mobile's first [racial segregation](/wiki/Racial_segregation "Racial segregation") ordinance, segregating the city streetcars. It legislated what had been informal practice, enforced by convention.[[51]](#cite_note-progress1-52) Mobile's African-American population responded to this with a two-month boycott, but the law was not repealed.[[51]](#cite_note-progress1-52) After this, Mobile's *de facto* segregation was increasingly replaced with legislated segregation as whites imposed [Jim Crow laws](/wiki/Jim_Crow_laws "Jim Crow laws") to maintain [supremacy](/wiki/White_supremacy "White supremacy").[[51]](#cite_note-progress1-52)

In 1911 the city adopted a commission form of government, which had three members elected by [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large") voting. Considered to be progressive, as it would reduce the power of ward bosses, this change resulted in the elite white majority strengthening its power, as only the majority could gain election of at-large candidates. In addition, poor whites and blacks had already been disenfranchised. Mobile was one of the last cities to retain this form of government, which prevented smaller groups from electing candidates of their choice. But Alabama's white yeomanry had historically favored [single-member districts](/wiki/Single-member_districts "Single-member districts") in order to elect candidates of their choice.[[52]](#cite_note-vra-53)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Alabama_-_Mobile_Bay_through_Mobile_-_NARA_-_23934849_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Alabama_-_Mobile_Bay_through_Mobile_-_NARA_-_23934849_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alabama_-_Mobile_Bay_through_Mobile_-_NARA_-_23934849_(cropped).jpg)

Warehouse district at the port, 1932

The [red imported fire ant](/wiki/Red_imported_fire_ant "Red imported fire ant") was first introduced into the United States via the Port of Mobile. Sometime in the late 1930s they came ashore off cargo ships arriving from South America. The ants were carried in the soil used as ballast on those ships.[[53]](#cite_note-54) They have spread throughout the South and Southwest.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Type_T2-SE-A1_tanker_Hat_Creek_underway_at_sea_on_16_August_1943.jpg/250px-Type_T2-SE-A1_tanker_Hat_Creek_underway_at_sea_on_16_August_1943.jpg)](/wiki/File:Type_T2-SE-A1_tanker_Hat_Creek_underway_at_sea_on_16_August_1943.jpg)

The [SS *Hat Creek*](/wiki/SS_Hat_Creek "SS Hat Creek"), a [T2 tanker](/wiki/T2_tanker "T2 tanker") completed by Alabama Drydock and Shipbuilding Company in 1943. The company built 102 of these oil tankers during WWII.

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the defense buildup in Mobile shipyards resulted in a considerable increase in the city's white middle-class and working-class population, largely due to the massive influx of workers coming to work in the shipyards and at [Brookley Army Air Field](/wiki/Brookley_Air_Force_Base "Brookley Air Force Base").[[55]](#cite_note-thomason2-56) Between 1940 and 1943, more than 89,000 people moved into Mobile to work for war effort industries.[[55]](#cite_note-thomason2-56)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Liberty_ship_at_sea.jpg/250px-Liberty_ship_at_sea.jpg)](/wiki/File:Liberty_ship_at_sea.jpg)

A [Liberty ship](/wiki/Liberty_ship "Liberty ship") of the type built at [Alabama Drydock and Shipbuilding Company](/wiki/Alabama_Drydock_and_Shipbuilding_Company "Alabama Drydock and Shipbuilding Company") during World War II. Twenty were completed in Mobile.

Mobile was one of eighteen United States cities producing [Liberty ships](/wiki/Liberty_ships "Liberty ships"). Its [Alabama Drydock and Shipbuilding Company](/wiki/Alabama_Drydock_and_Shipbuilding_Company "Alabama Drydock and Shipbuilding Company") (ADDSCO) supported the war effort by producing ships faster than the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers_of_World_War_II "Axis powers of World War II") could sink them. ADDSCO also churned out a copious number of [T2 tankers](/wiki/T2_tanker "T2 tanker") for the War Department.[[55]](#cite_note-thomason2-56) [Gulf Shipbuilding Corporation](/wiki/Gulf_Shipbuilding_Corporation "Gulf Shipbuilding Corporation"), a subsidiary of [Waterman Steamship Corporation](/wiki/Waterman_Steamship_Corporation "Waterman Steamship Corporation"), focused on building [freighters](/wiki/Cargo_ship "Cargo ship"), [*Fletcher*-class](/wiki/Fletcher-class_destroyer "Fletcher-class destroyer") [destroyers](/wiki/Destroyer "Destroyer"), and [minesweepers](/wiki/Minesweeper_(ship) "Minesweeper (ship)").[[55]](#cite_note-thomason2-56) The rapid increase of population in the city produced crowded conditions, increasing social tensions in the competition for housing and good jobs.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

In May 1943, a [race riot](/wiki/Race_riot "Race riot") broke out between whites and blacks. ADDSCO management had long maintained segregated conditions at the shipyards, although the Roosevelt administration had ordered defense contractors to integrate facilities. That year ADDSCO promoted 12 blacks to positions as welders, previously reserved for whites; and whites objected to the change by rioting on May 24. The mayor appealed to the governor to call in the [National Guard](/wiki/National_Guard_(United_States) "National Guard (United States)") to restore order, but it was weeks before officials allowed African Americans to return to work.[[57]](#cite_note-addsco-58)

### Post-WWII

In the late 1940s, the transition to the postwar economy was hard for the city, as thousands of jobs were lost at the shipyards with the decline in the defense industry. Eventually the city's social structure began to become more liberal. Replacing shipbuilding as a primary economic force, the paper and chemical industries began to expand. No longer needed for defense, most of the old military bases were converted to civilian uses. Following the war, in which many African Americans had served, veterans and their supporters stepped up activism to gain enforcement of their constitutional rights and social justice, especially in the [Jim Crow](/wiki/Jim_Crow "Jim Crow") South. During the 1950s the City of Mobile integrated its police force and [Spring Hill College](/wiki/Spring_Hill_College "Spring Hill College") accepted students of all races. Unlike in the rest of the state, by the early 1960s the city buses and lunch counters voluntarily desegregated.[[55]](#cite_note-thomason2-56)

The Alabama legislature passed the Cater Act in 1949, allowing cities and counties to set up industrial development boards (IDB) to issue municipal bonds as incentives to attract new industry into their local areas. The city of Mobile did not establish a Cater Act board until 1962. [George E. McNally](/wiki/George_E._McNally "George E. McNally"), Mobile's first Republican mayor since Reconstruction, was the driving force behind the founding of the IDB. The Mobile Area Chamber of Commerce, believing its members were better qualified to attract new businesses and industry to the area, considered the new IDB as a serious rival. After several years of political squabbling, the Chamber of Commerce emerged victorious. While McNally's IDB prompted the Chamber of Commerce to become more proactive in attracting new industry, the chamber effectively shut Mobile city government out of economic development decisions.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

In 1963, three African-American students brought a case against the Mobile County School Board for being denied admission to [Murphy High School](/wiki/Murphy_High_School,_Alabama "Murphy High School, Alabama").[[59]](#cite_note-murphy1-60) This was nearly a decade after the United States Supreme Court had ruled in *[Brown v. Board of Education](/wiki/Brown_v._Board_of_Education "Brown v. Board of Education")* (1954) that segregation of public schools was unconstitutional. The federal district court ordered that the three students be admitted to Murphy for the 1964 school year, leading to the desegregation of Mobile County's school system.[[59]](#cite_note-murphy1-60)

The [civil rights movement](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement") gained congressional passage of the [Civil Rights Act of 1964](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1964 "Civil Rights Act of 1964") and [Voting Rights Act of 1965](/wiki/Voting_Rights_Act_of_1965 "Voting Rights Act of 1965"), which increased the percentage of black residents able to vote,[[60]](#cite_note-61) ended multiple forms of segregation, and allowed the NAACP to return to Mobile.[[61]](#cite_note-62) However, the city's commission form of government with [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large") voting resulted in all positions being elected by the white majority, as African Americans could not command a majority for their candidates in the informally segregated city.[[62]](#cite_note-63) Many forms of [de facto segregation](/wiki/De_facto#Segregation "De facto") persisted for decades.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Downtown_Mobile_2008_03.JPG/250px-Downtown_Mobile_2008_03.JPG)](/wiki/File:Downtown_Mobile_2008_03.JPG)

Downtown in 2008, as seen from Cooper Riverside Park. Buildings include (L to R): The Renaissance Mobile Riverview Plaza Hotel, [RSA–BankTrust Building](/wiki/RSA%E2%80%93BankTrust_Building "RSA–BankTrust Building"), Arthur C. Outlaw Convention Center, and the [RSA Battle House Tower](/wiki/RSA_Battle_House_Tower "RSA Battle House Tower").

In 1969, the Department of Defense closed [Brookley Air Force Base](/wiki/Brookley_Air_Force_Base "Brookley Air Force Base"), dealing a blow to Mobile's economy. It affected about 10% of workers in the city.[[64]](#cite_note-65) In total, 16,000 people lost their jobs.[[65]](#cite_note-66)

Mobile's city commission form of government was challenged and finally overturned in 1982 in *[City of Mobile v. Bolden](/wiki/City_of_Mobile_v._Bolden "City of Mobile v. Bolden")*, which was remanded by the [United States Supreme Court](/wiki/United_States_Supreme_Court "United States Supreme Court") to the district court. Finding that the city had adopted a commission form of government in 1911 and [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large") positions with discriminatory intent, the court proposed that the three members of the city commission should be elected from [single-member districts](/wiki/Single-member_districts "Single-member districts"), likely ending their division of executive functions among them. Mobile's state legislative delegation in 1985 finally enacted a [mayor-council](/wiki/Mayor-council "Mayor-council") form of government, with seven members elected from [single-member districts](/wiki/Single-member_districts "Single-member districts"). This was approved by voters.[[52]](#cite_note-vra-53) As white conservatives increasingly entered the Republican Party in the late 20th century, African-American residents of the city have elected members of the Democratic Party as their candidates of choice. Since the change to single-member districts, more women and African Americans were elected to the council than under the at-large system.[[52]](#cite_note-vra-53)

Beginning in the late 1980s, newly elected mayor [Mike Dow](/wiki/Mike_Dow "Mike Dow") and the city council began an effort termed the "String of Pearls Initiative" to make Mobile into a competitive city.[[66]](#cite_note-progress2-67) The city initiated construction of numerous new facilities and projects, and the restoration of hundreds of historic downtown buildings and homes.[[66]](#cite_note-progress2-67) City and county leaders also made efforts to attract new business ventures to the area.[[67]](#cite_note-progress3-68)

## Geography

Mobile is located in the southwestern region of the U.S. state of Alabama.[[68]](#cite_note-GR1-69)

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 180.07 square miles (466.4 km2), with 139.48 square miles (361.3 km2) of it being land, and 40.59 square miles (105.1 km2), or 22.5% of the total, being covered by water.[[4]](#cite_note-CenPopGazetteer2023-5) The elevation in Mobile ranges from 10 feet (3 m) on Water Street in downtown[[8]](#cite_note-gnis-9) to 211 feet (64 m) at the Mobile Regional Airport.[[69]](#cite_note-elevationcham-70)

### Neighborhoods

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Ashland_Place_Mobile_AL_05.JPG/250px-Ashland_Place_Mobile_AL_05.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ashland_Place_Mobile_AL_05.JPG)

A Tudor Revival-style house in [Ashland Place](/wiki/Ashland_Place_Historic_District_(Mobile,_Alabama) "Ashland Place Historic District (Mobile, Alabama)")

Mobile has a number of notable historic neighborhoods. These include [Ashland Place](/wiki/Ashland_Place_Historic_District_(Mobile,_Alabama) "Ashland Place Historic District (Mobile, Alabama)"), [Campground](/wiki/Campground_Historic_District "Campground Historic District"), [Church Street East](/wiki/Church_Street_East_Historic_District "Church Street East Historic District"), [De Tonti Square](/wiki/De_Tonti_Square_Historic_District "De Tonti Square Historic District"), [Leinkauf](/wiki/Leinkauf_Historic_District "Leinkauf Historic District"), [Lower Dauphin Street](/wiki/Lower_Dauphin_Street_Historic_District "Lower Dauphin Street Historic District"), [Midtown](/wiki/Midtown_Historic_District_(Mobile,_Alabama) "Midtown Historic District (Mobile, Alabama)"), [Oakleigh Garden](/wiki/Oakleigh_Garden_Historic_District "Oakleigh Garden Historic District"), [Old Dauphin Way](/wiki/Old_Dauphin_Way_Historic_District "Old Dauphin Way Historic District"), [Spring Hill](/wiki/Spring_Hill_(Mobile,_Alabama) "Spring Hill (Mobile, Alabama)"), and [Toulminville](/wiki/Toulminville "Toulminville").[[70]](#cite_note-71)[[71]](#cite_note-72)[[72]](#cite_note-73)

### Climate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/KatrinaMobileCourthouseSteps.jpg/250px-KatrinaMobileCourthouseSteps.jpg)](/wiki/File:KatrinaMobileCourthouseSteps.jpg)

Flooding at the federal courthouse on Saint Joseph Street, three blocks from the waterfront, during [Hurricane Katrina](/wiki/Hurricane_Katrina "Hurricane Katrina") in 2005

Mobile's geographical location on the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico") provides a mild [subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Cfa*), with hot, humid summers and mild, rainy winters. The record low temperature was −1 °F (−18 °C), which was set on February 13, 1899.[[73]](#cite_note-Feb_Averages-74) The record high temperature is 106 °F (41 °C), which was set on 26 August 2023.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

A 2007 study determined that Mobile is the wettest city in the contiguous 48 states, with 66.3 inches (1,680 mm) of average annual rainfall over a 30-year period.[[75]](#cite_note-weatherbill-76) Mobile averages 120 days per year with at least 0.01 inches (0.3 mm) of rain. Precipitation is heavy year-round. On average, July and August are the wettest months, with frequent and often-heavy shower and thunderstorm activity. October is slightly drier than other months. [Snow](/wiki/Snow "Snow") is rare in Mobile. From 1996 to 2017, the city did not see snowfall.[[76]](#cite_note-77)
The most recent snowfall event occurred [January 21, 2025](/wiki/January_20%E2%80%9322,_2025_Gulf_Coast_blizzard "January 20–22, 2025 Gulf Coast blizzard"), which produced record-breaking accumulations of up to 8.5 inches and near-blizzard conditions.[[77]](#cite_note-78)[[78]](#cite_note-79) The snowfall event previous to this one was on January 16–17, 2018.[[79]](#cite_note-80)

Mobile has been affected by major tropical storms and hurricanes.[[16]](#cite_note-srg-17) The city suffered a major natural disaster on the night of September 12, 1979, when [Category-3](/wiki/Saffir%E2%80%93Simpson_Hurricane_Scale "Saffir–Simpson Hurricane Scale") [Hurricane Frederic](/wiki/Hurricane_Frederic "Hurricane Frederic") passed over the heart of the city. The storm caused tremendous damage to Mobile and the surrounding area.[[80]](#cite_note-hurricane1-81) Mobile had moderate damage from [Hurricane Opal](/wiki/Hurricane_Opal "Hurricane Opal") on October 4, 1995, and [Hurricane Ivan](/wiki/Hurricane_Ivan "Hurricane Ivan") on September 16, 2004.[[81]](#cite_note-HurIvan-82)

Mobile suffered millions of dollars in damage from [Hurricane Katrina](/wiki/Hurricane_Katrina "Hurricane Katrina") on August 29, 2005, which damaged many Gulf Coast cities. A storm surge of 11.45 feet (3.49 m), topped by higher waves, damaged eastern sections of the city with extensive flooding in downtown, the [Battleship Parkway](/wiki/Battleship_Parkway "Battleship Parkway"), and the elevated [Jubilee Parkway](/wiki/Jubilee_Parkway "Jubilee Parkway").[[82]](#cite_note-HurKat-83)

In 2020, on the 16th anniversary of Ivan, [Hurricane Sally](/wiki/Hurricane_Sally "Hurricane Sally") became the first Hurricane since Ivan to directly hit Alabama. Although a [Category-2](/wiki/Saffir%E2%80%93Simpson_scale "Saffir–Simpson scale"), Sally moved slowly through Alabama causing significant damage in its path. While the area was still recovering from Sally, a month later [Hurricane Zeta](/wiki/Hurricane_Zeta "Hurricane Zeta") struck the area as a [Category-3](/wiki/Saffir%E2%80%93Simpson_scale "Saffir–Simpson scale").[[83]](#cite_note-84)

#### Christmas Day tornado

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/House_on_Springhill_Avenue_Mobile_12-29-2012.jpg/250px-House_on_Springhill_Avenue_Mobile_12-29-2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:House_on_Springhill_Avenue_Mobile_12-29-2012.jpg)

A house on Springhill Avenue destroyed in the Christmas Day 2012 tornado

See also: [Late December 2012 North American storm complex](/wiki/Late_December_2012_North_American_storm_complex "Late December 2012 North American storm complex")

In December 2012, the city suffered two major tornadoes. One touched down on December 20, and the other five days later.[[90]](#cite_note-presstornado-92) On December 20, an EF1 tornado touched down near [Davidson High School](/wiki/Davidson_High_School_(Mobile,_Alabama) "Davidson High School (Mobile, Alabama)") and took a path ending in Prichard.[[91]](#cite_note-93) On December 25, 2012, at 4:54 pm, a large [wedge tornado](/wiki/Wedge_tornado "Wedge tornado") touched down.[[90]](#cite_note-presstornado-92) It rapidly intensified as it moved north-northeast at speeds of up to 50 mph (80 km/h), causing damage or destruction to at least 100 structures in [Midtown](/wiki/Midtown_Historic_District_(Mobile,_Alabama) "Midtown Historic District (Mobile, Alabama)"). The heaviest damage to houses was along Carlen Street, Rickarby Place, Dauphin Street, [Old Shell Road](/wiki/Old_Shell_Road "Old Shell Road"), Margaret Street, Silverwood Street, and Springhill Avenue.[[90]](#cite_note-presstornado-92) The second tornado was classified as an [EF2](/wiki/Enhanced_Fujita_scale "Enhanced Fujita scale") tornado by the [National Weather Service](/wiki/National_Weather_Service "National Weather Service") on December 26.[[90]](#cite_note-presstornado-92) As a result of the significant damage from the tornado, [Murphy High School](/wiki/Murphy_High_School_(Alabama) "Murphy High School (Alabama)") students were transferred to nearby [Clark-Shaw Magnet School](/wiki/Mobile_County_Public_School_System#Middle "Mobile County Public School System") to finish out the school year as repairs were being made to Murphy High. This increased the student body at the Clark-Shaw campus from 700 students to almost 3,000 students.[[92]](#cite_note-94)

As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), there were 187,041 people, 77,772 households, and 45,953 families residing in the city.[[96]](#cite_note-98) The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 1,341.0 inhabitants per square mile (517.8/km2).[[97]](#cite_note-99) There were 89,215 housing units. The racial makeup of the city was 40.12% White, 51.06% Black or African American, 0.27% Native American, 1.80% Asian, 0.06% Pacific Islander, and 3.13% from two or more races. Hispanic or Latino people of any race were 3.23% of the population.[[98]](#cite_note-100) After annexing areas west of the city in July 2023, Mobile's population increased to 204,689 residents, making it the [second-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Alabama "List of municipalities in Alabama") in Alabama with only [Huntsville](/wiki/Huntsville,_Alabama "Huntsville, Alabama") having a larger population.[[99]](#cite_note-:0-101) The annexation shifted racial demographics; Mobile became a [majority-minority](/wiki/Majority-minority "Majority-minority") city with Black or African American residents remaining the largest racial group.[[100]](#cite_note-102)

According to [American Values Atlas](/wiki/American_Values_Atlas "American Values Atlas") data published in 2014, the majority of the population were Christians, with 36% identifying as white evangelical Protestant, 18% identifying as black Protestant, 13% as [mainline Protestant](/wiki/Mainline_Protestant "Mainline Protestant"), and 7% as Catholic. 14% of the population identified as unaffiliated with any religion.[[101]](#cite_note-103) According to the 2024 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") estimates, 19.7% of the population was under 18. The median age was 38.6. The average family size was 3.13 people. The [median household income](/wiki/Median_income "Median income") in Mobile was $50,156, while the median income for a family was $73,717. 15.2% of the population were living below the poverty line.[[102]](#cite_note-104)

## Economy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Mobile_River_at_Chickasaw_Creek.jpg/250px-Mobile_River_at_Chickasaw_Creek.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mobile_River_at_Chickasaw_Creek.jpg)

Port of Mobile at Chickasaw Creek

Aerospace, steel, ship building, retail, services, construction, medicine, and manufacturing are Mobile's major industries. After several decades of economic decline, Mobile's economy began to rebound in the late 1980s. Between 1993 and 2003 roughly 13,983 new jobs were created as 87 new companies were founded and 399 existing companies were expanded.[[106]](#cite_note-MobEconomy-108)

Defunct companies that had been founded or based in Mobile included [Alabama Drydock and Shipbuilding Company](/wiki/Alabama_Drydock_and_Shipbuilding_Company "Alabama Drydock and Shipbuilding Company"), [Delchamps](/wiki/Delchamps "Delchamps"), and [Gayfers](/wiki/Gayfers "Gayfers").[[107]](#cite_note-bizjournal-109)[[108]](#cite_note-delchamps-110)[[109]](#cite_note-Flottes-Gayfers-111) Current companies that were formerly based in the city include [Checkers](/wiki/Checkers_(fast_food) "Checkers (fast food)"), [Minolta-QMS](/wiki/Konica_Minolta "Konica Minolta"), [Morrison's](/wiki/Morrison_Management_Specialists "Morrison Management Specialists"), and the [Waterman Steamship Corporation](/wiki/Waterman_Steamship_Corporation "Waterman Steamship Corporation").[[110]](#cite_note-checkers-112)[[111]](#cite_note-113) In addition to those discussed below, AlwaysHD, [Foosackly's](/wiki/Foosackly%27s "Foosackly's"), [Integrity Media](/wiki/Integrity_Media "Integrity Media"), and [Volkert, Inc.](/wiki/Volkert,_Inc. "Volkert, Inc.") are headquartered in Mobile.[[112]](#cite_note-alwayshd-114)[[113]](#cite_note-foosack-115)[[114]](#cite_note-116)[[115]](#cite_note-volkerthistory-117)

### Major industry

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Port_of_Mobile_Cranes.jpg/250px-Port_of_Mobile_Cranes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Port_of_Mobile_Cranes.jpg)

Cranes at the Port of Mobile container terminal

#### Port of Mobile and shipyards

In 2005, Mobile's [Alabama State Docks](/wiki/Port_of_Mobile "Port of Mobile") completed the largest expansion in its history, increasing its container processing and storage facility, and its container storage at the docks by over 1,000% at a cost of over $300 million.[[116]](#cite_note-118) Despite the expansion and addition of two massive new cranes, the port went from 9th largest to the 12th largest by tonnage in the nation from 2008 to 2010.[[13]](#cite_note-port-14)[[117]](#cite_note-ports2-119)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/US_Navy_110908-O-ZZ999-003_The_Military_Sealift_Command_joint_high_speed_vessel_USNS_Spearhead_%28JHSV_1%29_prepares_for_its_Sept._17_christening_cerem.jpg/250px-US_Navy_110908-O-ZZ999-003_The_Military_Sealift_Command_joint_high_speed_vessel_USNS_Spearhead_%28JHSV_1%29_prepares_for_its_Sept._17_christening_cerem.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Navy_110908-O-ZZ999-003_The_Military_Sealift_Command_joint_high_speed_vessel_USNS_Spearhead_(JHSV_1)_prepares_for_its_Sept._17_christening_cerem.jpg)

The [USNS *Spearhead* (JHSV-1)](/wiki/USNS_Spearhead "USNS Spearhead"), another Austal USA-built ship, being prepared for its christening in the [BAE Systems Southeast Shipyards](/wiki/BAE_Systems_Southeast_Shipyards "BAE Systems Southeast Shipyards") floating drydock in September 2011. The *Spearhead* is the first ship of the [*Spearhead* class Joint High Speed Vessel (JHSV)](/wiki/Spearhead_class_Joint_High_Speed_Vessel "Spearhead class Joint High Speed Vessel").

Shipbuilding increased substantially in 1999 with the founding of [Austal USA](/wiki/Austal_USA "Austal USA"),[[118]](#cite_note-colton1-120) expanding its production facility for United States defense and commercial aluminum shipbuilding on [Blakeley Island](/wiki/Blakeley_Island_(Alabama) "Blakeley Island (Alabama)") in 2005.[[119]](#cite_note-austal-121) [Atlantic Marine](/wiki/Atlantic_Marine "Atlantic Marine") operated a major shipyard at the former Alabama Drydock and Shipbuilding Company site on [Pinto Island](/wiki/Pinto_Island "Pinto Island"). It was acquired by British defense conglomerate [BAE Systems](/wiki/BAE_Systems "BAE Systems") in May 2010 and renamed [BAE Systems Southeast Shipyards](/wiki/BAE_Systems_Southeast_Shipyards "BAE Systems Southeast Shipyards"). The company operates the site as a full-service shipyard, employing approximately 600 workers.[[107]](#cite_note-bizjournal-109)[[120]](#cite_note-us2010-122)[[121]](#cite_note-bae1-123)

#### Mobile Aeroplex at Brookley

The [Mobile Aeroplex at Brookley](/wiki/Mobile_Aeroplex_at_Brookley "Mobile Aeroplex at Brookley") is an industrial complex and airport located 3 miles (5 km) south of the central business district of the city. It is the largest industrial and transportation complex in the region with more than 70 companies, many of which are [aerospace](/wiki/Aerospace "Aerospace"), spread over 1,650 acres (668 ha).[[122]](#cite_note-overview-124) Notable employers at Brookley include [Airbus](/wiki/Airbus "Airbus") North America Engineering ([Airbus Military North America](/wiki/EADS_North_America "EADS North America")'s facilities are at the Mobile Regional Airport), [VT Mobile Aerospace Engineering](/wiki/VT_Mobile_Aerospace_Engineering "VT Mobile Aerospace Engineering") (a division of [ST Engineering](/wiki/ST_Engineering "ST Engineering")), and [Continental Motors](/wiki/Continental_Motors,_Inc. "Continental Motors, Inc.").[[123]](#cite_note-tenants-125)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Airbus_Mobile_Engineering_Center.jpg/250px-Airbus_Mobile_Engineering_Center.jpg)](/wiki/File:Airbus_Mobile_Engineering_Center.jpg)

Airbus Mobile Engineering Center at the Brookley Aeroplex in Mobile

An [Airbus A320 family](/wiki/Airbus_A320_family "Airbus A320 family") aircraft assembly plant, their first in the United States, was opened in Mobile in 2015 for the assembly of the A319, A320 and A321 aircraft. In 2017 it produced up to 50 aircraft per year.[[124]](#cite_note-apconfirm-126)[[125]](#cite_note-bbcconfirm-127)[[126]](#cite_note-128) In August 2019, the assembly plant began production on the Airbus A220 model.[[127]](#cite_note-129)[[128]](#cite_note-Airbus20190805-130)
