## Arts and culture

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Azalea_Trail_Maids.jpg/250px-Azalea_Trail_Maids.jpg)](/wiki/File:Azalea_Trail_Maids.jpg)

The [Azalea Trail Maids](/wiki/Azalea_Trail_Maids "Azalea Trail Maids") on the campus of [Spring Hill College](/wiki/Spring_Hill_College "Spring Hill College")

Unlike other Alabama cities, Mobile's French and Spanish colonial history has given it a culture distinguished by French, Spanish, [Creole](/wiki/Alabama_Creole_people "Alabama Creole people"), African, and Catholic heritage, in addition to later British and American influences. The annual [Carnival](/wiki/Carnival "Carnival") celebration, [Mardi Gras](/wiki/Mardi_Gras_in_Mobile,_Alabama "Mardi Gras in Mobile, Alabama"), is an example of its differences. Mobile has the longest history of celebrating [Mardi Gras](/wiki/Mardi_Gras "Mardi Gras") in the United States, dating to the early 18th century during the French colonial period.[[132]](#cite_note-LOCgras-134) Carnival in Mobile evolved over 300 years from a sedate French Catholic tradition to a mainstream multi-week celebration.[[133]](#cite_note-carnival4-135) Mobile's official cultural ambassadors are the [Azalea Trail Maids](/wiki/Azalea_Trail_Maids "Azalea Trail Maids"), meant to embody the ideals of [Southern hospitality](/wiki/Southern_hospitality "Southern hospitality").[[134]](#cite_note-azaleatrail-136)

### Carnival and Mardi Gras

See also: [Mardi Gras in Mobile, Alabama](/wiki/Mardi_Gras_in_Mobile,_Alabama "Mardi Gras in Mobile, Alabama") and [Mystic society](/wiki/Mystic_society "Mystic society")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Mobile_Order_of_Incas_parade_03.jpg/250px-Mobile_Order_of_Incas_parade_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mobile_Order_of_Incas_parade_03.jpg)

Order of Inca night parade in 2009

The Carnival season has expanded throughout the late fall and winter: [balls](/wiki/Ball_(dance) "Ball (dance)") in the city may be scheduled as early as November, with the [parades](/wiki/Parade "Parade") beginning after January 5 and the Twelfth Day of Christmas or Epiphany on January 6.[[135]](#cite_note-mgrasfaqs-137)[[136]](#cite_note-carnivalterminology-138) Carnival celebrations end at midnight on [Mardi Gras](/wiki/Mardi_Gras "Mardi Gras"), which falls on the Tuesday before [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday").[[137]](#cite_note-slacabamorinico-139) During the Carnival season, [mystic societies](/wiki/Mystic_society "Mystic society") build floats, parade through downtown, and toss small gifts to spectators.[[138]](#cite_note-140) They also hold formal [masquerade balls](/wiki/Masquerade_ball "Masquerade ball"), usually by invitation only.[[136]](#cite_note-carnivalterminology-138)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Mobile_Mardi_Gras_2010_48.jpg/250px-Mobile_Mardi_Gras_2010_48.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mobile_Mardi_Gras_2010_48.jpg)

Knights of Revelry parade on Royal Street in 2010

Carnival was first celebrated in Mobile in 1703 when colonial French Catholic settlers carried out their traditional celebration at the [Old Mobile Site](/wiki/Old_Mobile_Site "Old Mobile Site").[[17]](#cite_note-MMtime-18) Mobile's first Carnival society was established in 1711 with the *Boeuf Gras Society* (Fatted Ox Society).[[139]](#cite_note-CarnHist1-141) In 1830 Mobile's Cowbellion de Rakin Society was the first formally organized and masked mystic society in the United States to celebrate with a parade.[[17]](#cite_note-MMtime-18)[[137]](#cite_note-slacabamorinico-139) The Cowbellions began their parade with rakes, hoes, and cowbells.[[137]](#cite_note-slacabamorinico-139) They introduced horse-drawn floats in 1840. The [Striker's Independent Society](/wiki/Striker%27s_Independent_Society "Striker's Independent Society"), formed in 1843, is the oldest surviving mystic society in the United States.[[139]](#cite_note-CarnHist1-141) Carnival celebrations were canceled during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War").[[140]](#cite_note-JChist-142)

Mardi Gras parades were revived by [Joe Cain](/wiki/Joe_Cain "Joe Cain") in the 1860s. He paraded as a fictional "undefeated Chickasaw chief" supported by a band of "[Lost Cause](/wiki/Lost_Cause_of_the_Confederacy "Lost Cause of the Confederacy") Minstrels" in defiance of [Union-led Reconstruction](/wiki/Reconstruction_era "Reconstruction era").[[141]](#cite_note-143) Founded in 2004, the Conde Explorers in 2005 were the first integrated Mardi Gras society to parade in downtown Mobile. The Explorers were featured in the documentary, *The Order of Myths* (2008), by [Margaret Brown](/wiki/Margaret_Brown_(film_director) "Margaret Brown (film director)") about Mobile's Mardi Gras.[[142]](#cite_note-PBS-144)[[143]](#cite_note-145)

### Archives and libraries

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Mobile_Public_Library_2008.jpg/250px-Mobile_Public_Library_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mobile_Public_Library_2008.jpg)

The Ben May Main Library on Government Street

The [National African American Archives and Museum](/wiki/National_African_American_Archives_and_Museum "National African American Archives and Museum") features the history of African-American participation in Mardi Gras, slavery-era artifacts, and portraits and biographies of famous African Americans.[[144]](#cite_note-naaam-146) The Doy Leale McCall Rare Book and Manuscript Library at the University of South Alabama are open to the public and house primary sources relating to the history of the university, Mobile, and southern Alabama.[[145]](#cite_note-usa1-147)[[146]](#cite_note-148) The Mobile Municipal Archives contains the extant city records, dating from the Mississippi Territory period.[[147]](#cite_note-cmma1-149) The Mobile Genealogical Society Library and Media Center features handwritten manuscripts and published materials that are available for use in genealogical research.[[148]](#cite_note-MGS_library-150)

The [Mobile Public Library](/wiki/Mobile_Public_Library "Mobile Public Library") system serves Mobile and consists of eight branches across Mobile County. Its local history and genealogy division is located near the [Ben May](/wiki/Ben_E._May "Ben E. May") Main Library on Government Street.[[149]](#cite_note-mpl1-151) The Saint Ignatius Archives, Museum and Theological Research Library contains primary sources, artifacts, documents, photographs and publications that pertain to the history of Saint Ignatius Church and School, the Catholic history of the city, and the history of the Roman Catholic Church.[[150]](#cite_note-siamt1-152)

### Arts and entertainment

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Mobile_Museum_of_Art_by_Highsmith.jpg/250px-Mobile_Museum_of_Art_by_Highsmith.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mobile_Museum_of_Art_by_Highsmith.jpg)

The Mobile Museum of Art in 2010

The [Mobile Museum of Art](/wiki/Mobile_Museum_of_Art "Mobile Museum of Art") features permanent exhibits that span several centuries of art and culture. The museum was expanded in 2002 to approximately 95,000 square feet (8,826 m2).[[151]](#cite_note-153) The Centre for the Living Arts is an organization that operates the historic [Saenger Theatre](/wiki/Saenger_Theatre_(Mobile,_Alabama) "Saenger Theatre (Mobile, Alabama)") and Space 301, a contemporary art gallery. The Saenger Theatre opened in 1927 as a [movie palace](/wiki/Movie_palace "Movie palace"). Today it is a performing arts center, a small concert venue, and home to the [Mobile Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Mobile_Symphony_Orchestra "Mobile Symphony Orchestra").[[152]](#cite_note-154) The [Crescent Theater](/wiki/Crescent_Theater "Crescent Theater") in downtown Mobile has shown [arthouse films](/wiki/Arthouse_film "Arthouse film") since 2008.[[153]](#cite_note-155)

The [Mobile Civic Center](/wiki/Mobile_Civic_Center "Mobile Civic Center") contains three facilities under one roof. The 400,000 sq ft (37,161 m2) building has an arena, a theater, and an exposition hall. It is the primary concert venue for the city and home to the [Mobile Opera](/wiki/Mobile_Opera "Mobile Opera") and Mobile Ballet.[[16]](#cite_note-srg-17) A variety of events are held at the Arthur C. Outlaw Convention Center.[[154]](#cite_note-acocc1-156)

The city has hosted the [Greater Gulf State Fair](/wiki/Greater_Gulf_State_Fair "Greater Gulf State Fair"), each October since 1955.[[155]](#cite_note-al-157) The city hosted [BayFest](/wiki/Bayfest_(Mobile) "Bayfest (Mobile)"), an annual three-day music festival, from 1995 to 2015.[[156]](#cite_note-bayfest1-158) Mobile also holds the Ten Sixty Five free music festival.[[157]](#cite_note-159)

The Mobile Chamber Music hosts various chamber musicians at the Laidlaw Performing Arts Center on the campus of the University of South Alabama and the Central Presbyterian Church.[[158]](#cite_note-160)[[159]](#cite_note-161)

The Mobile Theatre Guild is a nonprofit [community theatre](/wiki/Community_theatre "Community theatre") that has served the city since 1947. It is a member of the [Mobile Arts Council](/wiki/Mobile_Arts_Council "Mobile Arts Council"),[[160]](#cite_note-162) an umbrella organization for the arts in Mobile.[[161]](#cite_note-163) Mobile is also host to the Joe Jefferson Players, Alabama's oldest continually running community theatre. The group debuted on December 17, 1947, and was named in honor of comedic actor [Joe Jefferson](/wiki/Joseph_Jefferson "Joseph Jefferson"), who spent part of his teenage years in Mobile.[[162]](#cite_note-164)

### Museums

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/USS_Alabama_Mobile%2C_Alabama_002.JPG/250px-USS_Alabama_Mobile%2C_Alabama_002.JPG)](/wiki/File:USS_Alabama_Mobile,_Alabama_002.JPG)

The [USS *Alabama*](/wiki/USS_Alabama_(BB-60) "USS Alabama (BB-60)") at [Battleship Memorial Park](/wiki/Battleship_Memorial_Park "Battleship Memorial Park")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Vincent-Doan_House.jpg/250px-Vincent-Doan_House.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vincent-Doan_House.jpg)

The Vincent-Doan House, home to the Mobile Medical Museum, is one of the oldest extant houses in the city.

[Battleship Memorial Park](/wiki/Battleship_Memorial_Park "Battleship Memorial Park") is a military park on the shore of Mobile Bay. It features the World War II era [battleship](/wiki/Battleship "Battleship") [USS *Alabama*](/wiki/USS_Alabama_(BB-60) "USS Alabama (BB-60)"), the World War II era [submarine](/wiki/Submarine "Submarine") [USS *Drum*](/wiki/USS_Drum_(SS-228) "USS Drum (SS-228)"), [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War") and [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") Memorials, and historical military equipment.[[163]](#cite_note-battleship-165) The Fort of Colonial Mobile is a reconstruction of the city's original Fort Condé, built on the original fort's footprint. It serves as the official welcome center and a colonial-era living history museum.[[28]](#cite_note-MoMfort-29)

The [History Museum of Mobile](/wiki/Old_City_Hall_(Mobile,_Alabama) "Old City Hall (Mobile, Alabama)") showcases centuries of local history in the [Old City Hall](/wiki/Old_City_Hall_(Mobile,_Alabama) "Old City Hall (Mobile, Alabama)").[[164]](#cite_note-southernmarket-166) The Phoenix Fire Museum in the restored Phoenix Volunteer Fire Company Number 6 building covers fire companies dating to 1838.[[165]](#cite_note-pfm1-167) The Mobile Police Department Museum chronicles the history of the city's law enforcement.[[166]](#cite_note-mpdm1-168) The Mobile Medical Museum in the French colonial-style Vincent-Doan House chronicles the history of medicine in the city.[[167]](#cite_note-mmm1-169) The [Mobile Carnival Museum](/wiki/Mobile_Carnival_Museum "Mobile Carnival Museum") houses the city's [Mardi Gras](/wiki/Mardi_Gras_in_Mobile "Mardi Gras in Mobile") history and memorabilia.[[168]](#cite_note-170)

The [Bragg-Mitchell Mansion](/wiki/Bragg-Mitchell_Mansion "Bragg-Mitchell Mansion") (1855), [Richards DAR House](/wiki/Richards_DAR_House "Richards DAR House") (1860), and the [Condé-Charlotte House](/wiki/Conde-Charlotte_House "Conde-Charlotte House") (1822) are [antebellum](/wiki/Antebellum_architecture "Antebellum architecture") house museums.[[169]](#cite_note-bmm1-171)[[170]](#cite_note-rdarhm1-172)[[171]](#cite_note-ccmh1-173) The [Oakleigh Historic Complex](/wiki/Oakleigh_Historic_Complex_(Mobile,_Alabama) "Oakleigh Historic Complex (Mobile, Alabama)") are three house museums that portray the daily lives of enslaved, working class, and upper-class people during the 19th century.[[172]](#cite_note-Oakleigh-174)

The [Gulf Coast Exploreum Science Center](/wiki/Gulf_Coast_Exploreum_Science_Center "Gulf Coast Exploreum Science Center") is a non-profit science center located in downtown.[[173]](#cite_note-exploreum-175) The [Dauphin Island Sea Lab](/wiki/Dauphin_Island_Sea_Lab "Dauphin Island Sea Lab") is located south of the city, on Dauphin Island near the mouth of [Mobile Bay](/wiki/Mobile_Bay "Mobile Bay").[[174]](#cite_note-nytimes-176) On the University of South Alabama campus is the USA Archaeology Museum hosting artifacts from throughout the Gulf Coast region.[[175]](#cite_note-177) Airbus has an aerospace museum named Flightworks.[[176]](#cite_note-178) In the old *[Press-Register](/wiki/Press-Register "Press-Register")* building, there is the Alabama Contemporary Art Center, which contains artworks by living artists.[[177]](#cite_note-179)[[178]](#cite_note-180)

### Historic architecture

See also: [National Register of Historic Places listings in Mobile, Alabama](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_listings_in_Mobile,_Alabama "National Register of Historic Places listings in Mobile, Alabama")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Carlen_House.JPG/250px-Carlen_House.JPG)](/wiki/File:Carlen_House.JPG)

The Carlen House is an example of the Creole cottage style found along the Gulf Coast

Mobile has surviving antebellum architectural examples of the [Creole cottage](/wiki/Creole_cottage "Creole cottage"), [Greek Revival](/wiki/Greek_Revival_architecture "Greek Revival architecture"), [Gothic Revival](/wiki/Gothic_Revival "Gothic Revival"), and [Italianate](/wiki/Italianate_architecture "Italianate architecture") styles.[[179]](#cite_note-nris2-181) The earliest homes and fortifications were simple wooden structures, elevated on [pilings](/wiki/Piling "Piling") driven into the frequently soaked soil. Early cottages, similar to those in other French settlements, were built as rows of two or three separate rooms each with a front and rear door that often opened onto an external porch running the length of the home. Kitchens were separate buildings. Some of these wooden structures rotted away in the 1700s.[[180]](#cite_note-Gould-1988-7-9-47-117-182) Fires in 1827 and 1839 destroyed the city's remaining wooden colonial architecture.[[181]](#cite_note-183)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Mobile_City_Hospital.JPG/250px-Mobile_City_Hospital.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mobile_City_Hospital.JPG)

Mobile City Hospital was built in the Greek Revival style.

Wood continued to be used for nearly all homes in the 1800s, and the colonial cottages evolved into what became known as the [Creole cottage](/wiki/Creole_cottage "Creole cottage") in the 1840s. Creole cottages, such as the [Carlen House](/wiki/Carlen_House "Carlen House"), have front and rear porches covered by the roof. They run parallel to the street, often with a chimney at either end.[[180]](#cite_note-Gould-1988-7-9-47-117-182) The 1827 fire and an economic boom in the 1830s allowed much of the city to be rebuilt, often in the optimistic and then-popular [Greek Revival](/wiki/Greek_Revival_architecture_in_North_America "Greek Revival architecture in North America") style. Structures like the [Mobile City Hospital](/wiki/Mobile_City_Hospital "Mobile City Hospital") were built on an imposing scale with round [doric columns](/wiki/Doric_columns "Doric columns") in their facades.[[182]](#cite_note-Gould-1988-p57-60-184) A simplified Gothic Revival style gained popularity in the 1850s, with [cresting](/wiki/Cresting_(architecture) "Cresting (architecture)") along some rooflines and simple [tracery](/wiki/Tracery "Tracery") framing the windows.[[183]](#cite_note-Gould-1988-p140-143-185) The [Italianate](/wiki/Italianate "Italianate") style emerged before the Civil War and thrived after it. Italianate detailing can be seen in surviving examples such as the [Martin Horst House](/wiki/Martin_Horst_House "Martin Horst House"), with its [cast iron](/wiki/Cast_iron "Cast iron") rails, elaborate exterior molding, bracketed [eaves](/wiki/Eaves "Eaves"), and [parapet](/wiki/Parapet "Parapet") around the [hipped roof](/wiki/Hipped_roof "Hipped roof").[[184]](#cite_note-Gould-1988-169-170-186) Italianate storefronts became common in downtown Mobile until they were surpassed by the Victorian style in the late 1800s.[[185]](#cite_note-Gould-1988-210-221-187) Victorian cottages with board-and-[batten](/wiki/Batten "Batten") siding also became popular in the late 1800s, but few survive.[[186]](#cite_note-Gould-1988-p173-188) The city's lone example of the [Egyptian Revival](/wiki/Egyptian_Revival "Egyptian Revival") style is the [Scottish Rite Temple](/wiki/Scottish_Rite_Temple_(Mobile,_Alabama) "Scottish Rite Temple (Mobile, Alabama)"), with Egyptian motifs, including obelisks and sphinxes.[[187]](#cite_note-Gould-1988-235-236-189) Other architectural styles in the city include [shotgun houses](/wiki/Shotgun_house "Shotgun house"), [Colonial Revival](/wiki/Colonial_Revival_architecture "Colonial Revival architecture"), [Tudor Revival](/wiki/Tudor_Revival_architecture "Tudor Revival architecture"), [Spanish Colonial Revival](/wiki/Spanish_Colonial_Revival_architecture "Spanish Colonial Revival architecture"), and [Beaux-Arts](/wiki/Beaux-Arts_architecture "Beaux-Arts architecture").[[179]](#cite_note-nris2-181)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/1_South_Royal_Street_Pincus_Building_Mobile_AL_02.JPG/250px-1_South_Royal_Street_Pincus_Building_Mobile_AL_02.JPG)](/wiki/File:1_South_Royal_Street_Pincus_Building_Mobile_AL_02.JPG)

The [Pincus Building](/wiki/Pincus_Building "Pincus Building") in the Lower Dauphin Street Historic District

The city's [historic districts](/wiki/Historic_districts_in_the_United_States "Historic districts in the United States") include: [Old Dauphin Way](/wiki/Old_Dauphin_Way_Historic_District "Old Dauphin Way Historic District"), [Oakleigh Garden](/wiki/Oakleigh_Garden_Historic_District "Oakleigh Garden Historic District"), [Lower Dauphin Street](/wiki/Lower_Dauphin_Street_Historic_District "Lower Dauphin Street Historic District"), [Leinkauf](/wiki/Leinkauf_Historic_District "Leinkauf Historic District"), [De Tonti Square](/wiki/De_Tonti_Square_Historic_District "De Tonti Square Historic District"), [Church Street East](/wiki/Church_Street_East_Historic_District "Church Street East Historic District"), [Ashland Place](/wiki/Ashland_Place_Historic_District_(Mobile,_Alabama) "Ashland Place Historic District (Mobile, Alabama)"), [Campground](/wiki/Campground_Historic_District "Campground Historic District"), and [Midtown](/wiki/Midtown_Historic_District_(Mobile,_Alabama) "Midtown Historic District (Mobile, Alabama)").[[179]](#cite_note-nris2-181) Many storefronts from the 1800s still stand along Lower Dauphin Street. Renovations to the ground floors of some buildings often leave the upper floors untouched.[[188]](#cite_note-Gould-1988-p212-190) The Oakleigh Garden District is centered around the most photographed house in Mobile, [Oakleigh](/wiki/Oakleigh_Historic_Complex_(Mobile,_Alabama) "Oakleigh Historic Complex (Mobile, Alabama)"). Many bracketed Italianate residences were later built around it.[[189]](#cite_note-Gould-1988-pp99-100-191-191)

The [Cathedral Basilica of the Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Cathedral_Basilica_of_the_Immaculate_Conception_(Mobile,_Alabama) "Cathedral Basilica of the Immaculate Conception (Mobile, Alabama)") was built on the colonial-era Campo Santo cemetery, of which no trace remains.[[190]](#cite_note-192) Several historic cemeteries were established shortly after the colonial era. The [Church Street Graveyard](/wiki/Church_Street_Graveyard "Church Street Graveyard") contains above-ground tombs and monuments spread over 4 acres (2 ha) and was founded in 1819.[[191]](#cite_note-Sledge-193) The nearby 120-acre (49 ha) [Magnolia Cemetery](/wiki/Magnolia_Cemetery_(Mobile,_Alabama) "Magnolia Cemetery (Mobile, Alabama)") was established in 1836 and served as Mobile's primary burial site during the 19th and early 20th centuries.[[192]](#cite_note-magnoliacem-194) Mobile's [Jewish](/wiki/Congregation_Sha%27arai_Shomayim_(Mobile,_Alabama) "Congregation Sha'arai Shomayim (Mobile, Alabama)") community dates back to the 1820s, and the city has two historic [Jewish cemeteries](/wiki/Jewish_cemetery "Jewish cemetery"), [Sha'arai Shomayim Cemetery](/wiki/Sha%27arai_Shomayim_Cemetery "Sha'arai Shomayim Cemetery") and [Ahavas Chesed Cemetery](/wiki/Ahavas_Chesed_Cemetery "Ahavas Chesed Cemetery"). Sha'arai Shomayim is the older of the two.[[193]](#cite_note-Sledge4-195)

## Sports

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Inaugural_South_Alabama_Jaguars_Football_Game%2C_Ladd%E2%80%93Peebles_Stadium%2C_2009.jpg/250px-Inaugural_South_Alabama_Jaguars_Football_Game%2C_Ladd%E2%80%93Peebles_Stadium%2C_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Inaugural_South_Alabama_Jaguars_Football_Game,_Ladd%E2%80%93Peebles_Stadium,_2009.jpg)

The first [South Alabama Jaguars](/wiki/South_Alabama_Jaguars "South Alabama Jaguars") football game at [Ladd–Peebles Stadium](/wiki/Ladd%E2%80%93Peebles_Stadium "Ladd–Peebles Stadium")

See also: [History of sports in Mobile, Alabama](/wiki/History_of_sports_in_Mobile,_Alabama "History of sports in Mobile, Alabama")

### Football

Football is the most popular spectator sport in the state.[[194]](#cite_note-196) Alabama has never had a top-level professional football team in the [NFL](/wiki/NFL "NFL"),[[195]](#cite_note-197) but Mobile is one of several Alabama cities with a college football tradition.[[196]](#cite_note-198) Mobile has been home to the [Senior Bowl](/wiki/Senior_Bowl "Senior Bowl") since 1951, featuring the best college seniors in [NCAA football](/wiki/College_football "College football").[[197]](#cite_note-srbowl-199)

Mobile is the home of two [football](/wiki/American_football "American football") stadiums. The [Ladd-Peebles Stadium](/wiki/Ladd-Peebles_Stadium "Ladd-Peebles Stadium") opened in 1948 and has a current capacity of 40,646, making it the [fourth-largest stadium in the state](/wiki/List_of_American_football_stadiums_by_capacity "List of American football stadiums by capacity").[[198]](#cite_note-ladd-200) [Hancock Whitney Stadium](/wiki/Hancock_Whitney_Stadium "Hancock Whitney Stadium") opened in 2020 on the campus of [University of South Alabama](/wiki/University_of_South_Alabama "University of South Alabama") and has a current capacity of 25,450.[[199]](#cite_note-201)

The [68 Ventures Bowl](/wiki/68_Ventures_Bowl "68 Ventures Bowl"), originally known as the Mobile Alabama Bowl and later the GMAC Bowl, GoDaddy.com Bowl, Dollar General Bowl, and LendingTree Bowl, has been played at Hancock Whitney Stadium since 2021. The game was originally played at Ladd–Peebles Stadium from 1999 to 2020. It features opponents from the [Sun Belt](/wiki/Sun_Belt_Conference "Sun Belt Conference") and [Mid-American](/wiki/Mid-American_Conference "Mid-American Conference") conferences.[[200]](#cite_note-202) Since 1988, Ladd–Peebles Stadium has hosted the Alabama-Mississippi All-Star Classic. The top graduating high school seniors from their respective states compete each June.[[201]](#cite_note-AMall-star-203)

The University of South Alabama in Mobile established a football team in 2007, which went undefeated in its 2009 inaugural season. Their program moved to Division I/FBS in 2013 as a member of the [Sun Belt Conference](/wiki/Sun_Belt_Conference "Sun Belt Conference"). The team currently plays at Hancock Whitney Stadium, after playing at Ladd-Peebles Stadium prior to the start of the 2020 Season.[[202]](#cite_note-football-204)

### Other sports and facilities

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Hank_Aaron_Stadium.jpg/250px-Hank_Aaron_Stadium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hank_Aaron_Stadium.jpg)

The [Hank Aaron Stadium](/wiki/Hank_Aaron_Stadium "Hank Aaron Stadium")

Mobile has been home to [Minor League Baseball](/wiki/Minor_League_Baseball "Minor League Baseball") teams from the late nineteenth century to 2019. Three [Southern League](/wiki/Southern_League_(1885%E2%80%931899) "Southern League (1885–1899)") teams operated out of Mobile intermittently in the nineteenth century: the Swamp Angels, Blackbirds, and Bluebirds. In the twentieth century, several teams, each called the Bears, operated at different times.[[203]](#cite_note-205) Mobile's [Hank Aaron Stadium](/wiki/Hank_Aaron_Stadium "Hank Aaron Stadium") was the home of the Minor League [Mobile BayBears](/wiki/Mobile_BayBears "Mobile BayBears") from 1997 to 2019.[[204]](#cite_note-baybears1-206)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Mitchell_center_north_entrance.jpg/250px-Mitchell_center_north_entrance.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mitchell_center_north_entrance.jpg)

Entrance to the Mitchell Center at the University of South Alabama

South Alabama basketball is a mid-major team in the Sun Belt Conference. They play their home games at the [Mitchell Center](/wiki/Mitchell_Center "Mitchell Center").[[205]](#cite_note-207) The Archbishop Lipscomb Athletic Complex is home of [AFC Mobile](/wiki/AFC_Mobile "AFC Mobile"), which is a [National Premier Soccer League](/wiki/National_Premier_Soccer_League "National Premier Soccer League") team.[[206]](#cite_note-208) The public Mobile Tennis Center includes over 50 courts, all lighted and hard-court.[[207]](#cite_note-Tennis-209)

For golfers, Magnolia Grove, part of the [Robert Trent Jones Golf Trail](/wiki/Robert_Trent_Jones_Golf_Trail "Robert Trent Jones Golf Trail"), has 36 holes. The Falls course was recently named the best par 3 course in America.[[208]](#cite_note-rtj-210) [The Mitchell Company Tournament of Champions](/wiki/The_Mitchell_Company_Tournament_of_Champions "The Mitchell Company Tournament of Champions") was played annually at Magnolia Grove from 1999 through 2007. The [Mobile Bay LPGA Classic](/wiki/Mobile_Bay_LPGA_Classic "Mobile Bay LPGA Classic") took its place in 2008, also held at Mobile's Magnolia Grove.[[209]](#cite_note-211)

Mobile is home to the [Azalea Trail Run](/wiki/Azalea_Trail_Run "Azalea Trail Run"), which races through historic midtown and downtown Mobile. This 10k run has been an annual event since 1978.[[210]](#cite_note-com-212) The Azalea Trail Run is one of the premier 10k road races in the United States, attracting runners from all over the world.[[211]](#cite_note-vulcan-213)

## Parks and recreation

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Ketchum_Fountain_in_Bienville_Square.jpg/250px-Ketchum_Fountain_in_Bienville_Square.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ketchum_Fountain_in_Bienville_Square.jpg)

Ketchum Fountain in the center of [Bienville Square](/wiki/Bienville_Square "Bienville Square")

The [Mobile Botanical Gardens](/wiki/Mobile_Botanical_Gardens "Mobile Botanical Gardens") feature a variety of flora spread over 100 acres (40 ha). It contains the Millie McConnell [Rhododendron](/wiki/Rhododendron "Rhododendron") Garden with 1,000 evergreen and native azaleas and the 30-acre (12 ha) [Longleaf Pine](/wiki/Longleaf_Pine "Longleaf Pine") Habitat.[[212]](#cite_note-botanicalgard-214) [Bellingrath Gardens and Home](/wiki/Bellingrath_Gardens_and_Home "Bellingrath Gardens and Home"), located on [Fowl River](/wiki/Fowl_River "Fowl River"), is a 65-acre (26 ha) [botanical garden](/wiki/Botanical_garden "Botanical garden") and historic 10,500-square-foot (975 m2) mansion that dates to the 1930s.[[213]](#cite_note-bellingrathgard-215) The 5 Rivers Delta Resource Center is a facility that allows visitors to learn about and access the [Mobile](/wiki/Mobile_River "Mobile River"), [Tensaw](/wiki/Tensaw_River "Tensaw River"), [Apalachee](/wiki/Apalachee_River_(Alabama) "Apalachee River (Alabama)"), [Middle](/wiki/Middle_River_(Alabama) "Middle River (Alabama)"), [Blakeley](/wiki/Blakeley_River "Blakeley River"), and [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_River_(Alabama) "Spanish River (Alabama)") rivers.[[214]](#cite_note-5_Rivers-216) It was established to serve as an easily accessible gateway to the [Mobile-Tensaw River Delta](/wiki/Mobile-Tensaw_River_Delta "Mobile-Tensaw River Delta").[[215]](#cite_note-5_Rivers_Facilities-217) It offers boat and adventure tours, a small theater, an exhibit hall, meeting facilities, walking trails, and a canoe and kayak landing.[[216]](#cite_note-5RDS-218)

Mobile has more than 45 public parks within its limits, with some that are of special note.[[217]](#cite_note-parksmob-219) [Bienville Square](/wiki/Bienville_Square "Bienville Square") is a historic park in the Lower Dauphin Street Historic District. It assumed its current form in 1850 and is named for Mobile's founder, Jean-Baptiste Le Moyne, Sieur de Bienville.[[218]](#cite_note-220) It was once the principal gathering place for residents, when the city was smaller, and remains popular today. [Cathedral Square](/wiki/Cathedral_Square_(Mobile,_Alabama) "Cathedral Square (Mobile, Alabama)") is a one-block performing arts park, also in the Lower Dauphin Street Historic District, which is overlooked by the [Cathedral Basilica of the Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Cathedral_Basilica_of_the_Immaculate_Conception,_Mobile "Cathedral Basilica of the Immaculate Conception, Mobile").[[219]](#cite_note-mainstreet-221)

Spanish Plaza is a downtown park that honors the Spanish phase of the city between 1780 and 1813. It features the *Arches of Friendship*, a fountain presented to Mobile by the city of [Málaga](/wiki/M%C3%A1laga "Málaga"), Spain.[[220]](#cite_note-spanplaza-222) [Langan Park](/wiki/Langan_(Municipal)_Park "Langan (Municipal) Park"), the largest of the parks at 720 acres (291 ha), features lakes, natural spaces, and contains the [Mobile Museum of Art](/wiki/Mobile_Museum_of_Art "Mobile Museum of Art"), Azalea City Golf Course, [Mobile Botanical Gardens](/wiki/Mobile_Botanical_Gardens "Mobile Botanical Gardens") and Playhouse in the Park.[[217]](#cite_note-parksmob-219)

## Government

See also: [List of mayors of Mobile, Alabama](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Mobile,_Alabama "List of mayors of Mobile, Alabama") and [2025 Mobile City Council election](/wiki/2025_Mobile_City_Council_election "2025 Mobile City Council election")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Government_Plaza_Mobile.JPG/250px-Government_Plaza_Mobile.JPG)](/wiki/File:Government_Plaza_Mobile.JPG)

[Government Plaza](/wiki/Mobile_Government_Plaza "Mobile Government Plaza") in Mobile, seat of government for the city and the county

Since 1985 the government of Mobile has consisted of a mayor and a seven-member [city council](/wiki/City_council "City council").[[221]](#cite_note-Cityofficials1-223) The council members are elected from each of the seven city council [single-member districts](/wiki/Single-member_districts "Single-member districts") (SMDs). A [supermajority](/wiki/Supermajority "Supermajority") of five votes is required to conduct most council business.[[222]](#cite_note-224)

This form of city government was chosen by the voters after the previous form of government, which had three city commissioners, each elected at-large, was ruled in 1975 to substantially dilute the minority vote and violate the [Voting Rights Act](/wiki/Voting_Rights_Act "Voting Rights Act") in *[Bolden v. City of Mobile](/wiki/Mobile_v._Bolden "Mobile v. Bolden")*. The three at-large commissioners each required a majority vote to win. Due to appeals, the case took time to reach settlement and establishment of a new electoral system.[[223]](#cite_note-gov1-225) Municipal elections are held every four years and are nonpartisan.[[224]](#cite_note-226)

![Map](https://maps.wikimedia.org/img/osm-intl,a,a,a,300x200.png?lang=en&domain=en.wikipedia.org&title=Mobile%2C_Alabama&revid=1345391502&groups=_693b5cac47213c22d7ddeea6f56c7c882bf28991&parser=legacy)

[Sam Jones](/wiki/Sam_Jones_(mayor) "Sam Jones (mayor)") was elected in 2005 as the first African-American mayor of Mobile. He was re-elected for a second term in 2009 without opposition.[[225]](#cite_note-sammayor-227) His administration continued the focus on downtown redevelopment and bringing industries to the city. He ran for a third term in 2013 but was defeated by [Sandy Stimpson](/wiki/Sandy_Stimpson "Sandy Stimpson"). Stimpson remained mayor until November 2, 2025.[[226]](#cite_note-228)[[227]](#cite_note-229) In 2025, [Spiro Cheriogotis](/wiki/Spiro_Cheriogotis "Spiro Cheriogotis") became the current mayor.[[228]](#cite_note-230)

Mobile city has 2,167 employees. Of the property tax paid in the city, 11% goes to the city, 32% goes to the county, 10% goes to the state, and 47% goes to the school districts. The city has a 5% sales tax. For 2024, the city received $281.7 million in sales tax, $34.5 million in property tax, and $90.1 million for services such as business licenses. The total revenue for the city was $514.3 million, and the total expenditures was $455.3 million for 2024.[[229]](#cite_note-231)

## Education

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Murphy_High_School.jpg/250px-Murphy_High_School.jpg)](/wiki/File:Murphy_High_School.jpg)

[Murphy High School](/wiki/Murphy_High_School_(Alabama) "Murphy High School (Alabama)") in Midtown, originally Mobile High School. It is one of the seventeen high schools run by the [Mobile County Public School System](/wiki/Mobile_County_Public_School_System "Mobile County Public School System").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Spring_Hill_College_Quad_01.JPG/250px-Spring_Hill_College_Quad_01.JPG)](/wiki/File:Spring_Hill_College_Quad_01.JPG)

Administration building at [Spring Hill College](/wiki/Spring_Hill_College "Spring Hill College")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Bishop_State_clock_tower_May_2012_cropped.jpg/250px-Bishop_State_clock_tower_May_2012_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bishop_State_clock_tower_May_2012_cropped.jpg)

The Business Technology Center and clock tower at [Bishop State Community College](/wiki/Bishop_State_Community_College "Bishop State Community College")

### Public schools

Public schools in Mobile are operated by the [Mobile County Public School System](/wiki/Mobile_County_Public_School_System "Mobile County Public School System") (MCPSS). MCPSS has an enrollment of approximately 52,000 students at 92 schools, employs approximately 7,200 public school employees,[[230]](#cite_note-232)[[231]](#cite_note-233) and had a budget in 2024–2025 of $843 million.[[232]](#cite_note-234) The State of Alabama operates the [Alabama School of Mathematics and Science](/wiki/Alabama_School_of_Mathematics_and_Science "Alabama School of Mathematics and Science") on Dauphin Street in Mobile, which boards advanced Alabama high school students. It was founded in 1989 to identify, challenge, and educate future leaders.[[233]](#cite_note-asms-235)

### Private schools

[UMS-Wright Preparatory School](/wiki/UMS-Wright_Preparatory_School "UMS-Wright Preparatory School") is an independent co-educational [preparatory school](/wiki/University-preparatory_school "University-preparatory school").[[234]](#cite_note-mpsr1-236) It assumed its current configuration in 1988, when the University Military School (founded 1893) and the Julius T. Wright School for Girls (1923) merged to form UMS-Wright.[[235]](#cite_note-237) Many [parochial schools](/wiki/Parochial_school "Parochial school") belong to the [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Mobile](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Mobile "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Mobile"), including [McGill-Toolen Catholic High School](/wiki/McGill-Toolen_Catholic_High_School "McGill-Toolen Catholic High School").[[234]](#cite_note-mpsr1-236) Protestant schools include [St. Paul's Episcopal School](/wiki/St._Paul%27s_Episcopal_School "St. Paul's Episcopal School") and [Faith Academy](/wiki/Faith_Academy_(Mobile,_Alabama) "Faith Academy (Mobile, Alabama)").[[234]](#cite_note-mpsr1-236)

### Universities and colleges

The [University of South Alabama](/wiki/University_of_South_Alabama "University of South Alabama") is a public, [doctoral](/wiki/Doctorate "Doctorate")-level university established in 1963.[[236]](#cite_note-usa2-238) [Faulkner University](/wiki/Faulkner_University "Faulkner University") is a four-year private [Church of Christ](/wiki/Churches_of_Christ "Churches of Christ")-affiliated university based in [Montgomery, Alabama](/wiki/Montgomery,_Alabama "Montgomery, Alabama"). Faulkner founded the Mobile campus in 1975 and offers bachelor's degrees[[237]](#cite_note-faulkner1-239) and associate degrees.[[238]](#cite_note-faulkner2-240) [Spring Hill College](/wiki/Spring_Hill_College "Spring Hill College"), chartered in 1830, was the first Catholic college in the southeastern United States and is the third oldest [Jesuit](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") college in the country.[[239]](#cite_note-springhill1-241) Spring Hill College is a four-year private college with graduate programs[[240]](#cite_note-springhill2-242) and undergraduate divisions.[[241]](#cite_note-springhill3-243)

Founded in 1927, [Bishop State Community College](/wiki/Bishop_State_Community_College "Bishop State Community College") is a public, [historically African American](/wiki/Historically_black_colleges_and_universities "Historically black colleges and universities"), [community college](/wiki/Community_colleges_in_the_United_States "Community colleges in the United States") with four campuses in Mobile.[[242]](#cite_note-bishop1-244) Several post-secondary [vocational](/wiki/Vocational_education "Vocational education") institutions have a campus in Mobile including [Fortis College](/wiki/Fortis_College "Fortis College"), [Virginia College](/wiki/Virginia_College "Virginia College"), [ITT Technical Institute](/wiki/ITT_Technical_Institute "ITT Technical Institute") and [Remington College](/wiki/Remington_College "Remington College").[[243]](#cite_note-college1-245)

## Media

See also: [Media in Mobile, Alabama](/wiki/Media_in_Mobile,_Alabama "Media in Mobile, Alabama")

Mobile's *[Press-Register](/wiki/Press-Register "Press-Register")* is Alabama's oldest active newspaper, first published in 1813.[[244]](#cite_note-NHreg-246) The paper focuses on [Mobile](/wiki/Mobile_County,_Alabama "Mobile County, Alabama") and [Baldwin](/wiki/Baldwin_County,_Alabama "Baldwin County, Alabama") counties and the city of Mobile, but also serves southwestern Alabama and southeastern Mississippi.[[244]](#cite_note-NHreg-246) Mobile's alternative newspaper is the *[Lagniappe](/wiki/Lagniappe_(newspaper) "Lagniappe (newspaper)")* which was founded on July 24, 2002.[[245]](#cite_note-lagniappe1-247) [Visit Mobile](/w/index.php?title=Visit_Mobile&action=edit&redlink=1 "Visit Mobile (page does not exist)") is a marketing agency website that is focused on tourism in Mobile.[[246]](#cite_note-248)

Mobile is served locally by several over-the-air television stations including [WKRG 5](/wiki/WKRG "WKRG") ([CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS")), [WALA 10](/wiki/WALA "WALA") ([Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company")), [WPMI 15](/wiki/WPMI-TV "WPMI-TV") ([Roar](/wiki/Roar_(TV_network) "Roar (TV network)")), [WMPV 21](/wiki/WMPV-TV "WMPV-TV") ([religious](/wiki/Religious_broadcasting "Religious broadcasting")), [WDPM 23](/wiki/WDPM-DT "WDPM-DT") (religious), [WEIQ 42](/wiki/Alabama_Public_Television "Alabama Public Television") ([PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS")), and [WFNA 55](/wiki/WFNA_(TV) "WFNA (TV)") ([The CW](/wiki/The_CW "The CW")).[[247]](#cite_note-tv1-249) The region is also served by [WEAR 3](/wiki/WEAR-TV "WEAR-TV") ([ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company"), with [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") on DT2), [WSRE 31](/wiki/WSRE "WSRE") (PBS), [WHBR 34](/wiki/WHBR_(TV) "WHBR (TV)") (religious), [WFGX 35](/wiki/WFGX "WFGX") ([MyNetworkTV](/wiki/MyNetworkTV "MyNetworkTV")), [WJTC 44](/wiki/WJTC "WJTC") (independent), [WFBD 48](/wiki/WFBD "WFBD") ([America One](/wiki/America_One "America One")), [WPAN 53](/wiki/WPAN "WPAN") ([Jewelry Television](/wiki/Jewelry_Television "Jewelry Television")), and [WAWD 58](/wiki/WAWD "WAWD") (independent), all out of the [Pensacola, Florida](/wiki/Pensacola,_Florida "Pensacola, Florida") area.[[247]](#cite_note-tv1-249) Mobile is part of the Mobile–Pensacola–[Fort Walton Beach](/wiki/Fort_Walton_Beach,_Florida "Fort Walton Beach, Florida") [designated market area](/wiki/Designated_market_area "Designated market area"), as defined by [Nielsen Media Research](/wiki/Nielsen_Media_Research "Nielsen Media Research"). The Mobile-Pensacola-Fort Walton Beach market is ranked 57th in the nation for the 2024–25 television season.[[248]](#cite_note-250)

A total of 43 FM radio stations and 12 AM radio stations are located around the Mobile area and provide signals sufficiently strong to serve Mobile.[[249]](#cite_note-251)
Fourteen [FM](/wiki/FM_broadcasting "FM broadcasting") radio stations transmit from Mobile: [WAVH](/wiki/WAVH "WAVH"), [WBHY](/wiki/WBHY-FM "WBHY-FM"), [WBLX](/wiki/WBLX-FM "WBLX-FM"), [WDLT](/wiki/WDLT-FM "WDLT-FM"), [WHIL](/wiki/WHIL_(FM) "WHIL (FM)"), [WKSJ](/wiki/WKSJ-FM "WKSJ-FM"), WKSJ-HD2, [WLVM](/wiki/WLVM "WLVM"), [WMXC](/wiki/WMXC "WMXC"), [WMXC](/wiki/WMXC "WMXC")-HD2, [WQUA](/wiki/WQUA "WQUA"), [WRKH](/wiki/WRKH "WRKH"), [WRKH](/wiki/WRKH "WRKH")-HD2, and [WZEW](/wiki/WZEW "WZEW"). Nine [AM](/wiki/AM_broadcasting "AM broadcasting") radio stations transmit from Mobile: [WBHY](/wiki/WBHY_(AM) "WBHY (AM)"), [WABF](/wiki/WABF_(AM) "WABF (AM)"), [WGOK](/wiki/WGOK "WGOK"), [WIJD](/wiki/WIJD "WIJD"), [WLPR](/wiki/WLPR-AM "WLPR-AM"), [WMOB](/wiki/WMOB "WMOB"), [WNGL](/wiki/WNGL "WNGL"), [WNTM](/wiki/WNTM "WNTM"), and [WXQW](/wiki/WXQW "WXQW"). The content ranges from Christian Contemporary to [Hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop "Hip hop") to [Top 40](/wiki/Top_40 "Top 40").[[250]](#cite_note-radio1-252) In fall 2025, [Nielsen](/wiki/Nielsen_Holdings "Nielsen Holdings") ranked Mobile's radio market as the 101st in the US.[[251]](#cite_note-253)

## Infrastructure

### Transportation

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Gulf_Mobile_%26_Ohio_Railroad_station.jpg/250px-Gulf_Mobile_%26_Ohio_Railroad_station.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gulf_Mobile_%26_Ohio_Railroad_station.jpg)

The old [Gulf, Mobile and Ohio Passenger Terminal](/wiki/Gulf,_Mobile_and_Ohio_Passenger_Terminal "Gulf, Mobile and Ohio Passenger Terminal") houses the Mobile Area Transportation Authority.

#### Air

Local airline passengers are served by the [Mobile Regional Airport](/wiki/Mobile_Regional_Airport "Mobile Regional Airport"), with direct connections to four major hub airports.[[252]](#cite_note-maa1-254) It is served by [American Eagle](/wiki/American_Eagle_(airline_brand) "American Eagle (airline brand)"), with service to [Dallas/Fort Worth International Airport](/wiki/Dallas/Fort_Worth_International_Airport "Dallas/Fort Worth International Airport") and [Charlotte/Douglas International Airport](/wiki/Charlotte/Douglas_International_Airport "Charlotte/Douglas International Airport"); [United Express](/wiki/United_Express "United Express"), with service to [George Bush Intercontinental Airport](/wiki/George_Bush_Intercontinental_Airport "George Bush Intercontinental Airport") and [Delta Connection](/wiki/Delta_Connection "Delta Connection"), with service to [Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport](/wiki/Hartsfield-Jackson_International_Airport "Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport").[[252]](#cite_note-maa1-254) The [Mobile Downtown Airport](/wiki/Mobile_Downtown_Airport "Mobile Downtown Airport") at the [Brookley Aeroplex](/wiki/Mobile_Aeroplex_at_Brookley "Mobile Aeroplex at Brookley") serves corporate, cargo, and private aircraft.[[252]](#cite_note-maa1-254)

#### Rail

Mobile is served by four [Class I railroads](/wiki/Class_I_railroad "Class I railroad"), including the [Canadian National Railway](/wiki/Canadian_National_Railway "Canadian National Railway") (CNR), [CSX Transportation](/wiki/CSX_Transportation "CSX Transportation") (CSX), the [Kansas City Southern Railway](/wiki/Kansas_City_Southern_Railway "Kansas City Southern Railway") (KCS), and the [Norfolk Southern Railway](/wiki/Norfolk_Southern_Railway "Norfolk Southern Railway") (NS).[[253]](#cite_note-mobilechamber-255) The [Alabama and Gulf Coast Railway](/wiki/Alabama_and_Gulf_Coast_Railway "Alabama and Gulf Coast Railway") (AGR), a [Class III railroad](/wiki/Class_III_railroad "Class III railroad"), links Mobile to the [Burlington Northern and Santa Fe Railway](/wiki/BNSF_Railway "BNSF Railway") (BNSF) at [Amory, Mississippi](/wiki/Amory,_Mississippi "Amory, Mississippi"). These converge at the Port of Mobile, which provides [intermodal freight transport](/wiki/Intermodal_freight_transport "Intermodal freight transport") service to companies engaged in importing and exporting. Other railroads include the [CG Railway](/wiki/CG_Railway "CG Railway") (CGR), a rail ship service to [Coatzacoalcos](/wiki/Coatzacoalcos "Coatzacoalcos"), [Veracruz](/wiki/Veracruz "Veracruz"), and the [Terminal Railway Alabama State Docks](/wiki/Terminal_Railway_Alabama_State_Docks "Terminal Railway Alabama State Docks") (TASD), a [switching railroad](/wiki/Switching_and_terminal_railroad "Switching and terminal railroad").[[253]](#cite_note-mobilechamber-255)

The city was served by [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak")'s *[Sunset Limited](/wiki/Sunset_Limited "Sunset Limited")* passenger train service until 2005, when the service was suspended due to the effects of [Hurricane Katrina](/wiki/Hurricane_Katrina "Hurricane Katrina").[[254]](#cite_note-Amtrak1-256)[[255]](#cite_note-Amtrak2-257) However, efforts to restart passenger rail service between Mobile and [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") were revived in 2019 by the 21-member Southern Rail Commission after receiving a $33 million Federal Railroad Administration grant in June of that year.[[256]](#cite_note-258) Louisiana quickly dedicated its $10 million toward the project, and Mississippi initially balked before committing its $15 million sum but Governor [Kay Ivey](/wiki/Kay_Ivey "Kay Ivey") resisted committing the estimated $2.7 million state allocation from Alabama because of concerns regarding long-term financial commitments and potential competition with freight traffic from the [Port of Mobile](/wiki/Port_of_Mobile "Port of Mobile").[[257]](#cite_note-259)

The Winter of 2019 was marked by repeated postponement of votes by the Mobile City Council as it requested more information on how rail traffic from the port would be impacted and where the Amtrak station would be built as community support for the project became more vocal, especially among [millennials](/wiki/Millennials "Millennials").[[258]](#cite_note-260) A day before a deadline in the federal grant matching program being used to fund the project, the city council committed about $3 million in a 6–1 vote.[[259]](#cite_note-261)

About $2.2 million was still needed for infrastructure improvements and the train station must still be built before service begins. Potential locations for the station include at the foot of [Government Street](/wiki/Government_Street_(Mobile,_Alabama) "Government Street (Mobile, Alabama)") in downtown and in the [Mobile Aeroplex at Brookley](/wiki/Mobile_Aeroplex_at_Brookley "Mobile Aeroplex at Brookley"), which was favored by the [Port of Mobile](/wiki/Port_of_Mobile "Port of Mobile").[[260]](#cite_note-262) Eventually, it was determined that a pocket track and a platform would be constructed for service to resume.[[261]](#cite_note-263) On July 1, 2025, Amtrak announced that the new train, dubbed the *[Mardi Gras Service](/wiki/Mardi_Gras_Service "Mardi Gras Service")*, would begin on August 18, 2025, with two daily return trips.[[262]](#cite_note-service_debut_announcement-264)

#### Roadways, bus and cycling

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/George_Wallace_Tunnel_02.jpg/250px-George_Wallace_Tunnel_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Wallace_Tunnel_02.jpg)

Interior of the eastbound [George Wallace Tunnel](/wiki/George_Wallace_Tunnel "George Wallace Tunnel") under the Mobile River

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Cochran_Bridge_Mobile_Alabama.jpg/250px-Cochran_Bridge_Mobile_Alabama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cochran_Bridge_Mobile_Alabama.jpg)

The [Cochrane–Africatown USA Bridge](/wiki/Cochrane%E2%80%93Africatown_USA_Bridge "Cochrane–Africatown USA Bridge") spanning the [Mobile River](/wiki/Mobile_River "Mobile River")

Two major [interstate highways](/wiki/Interstate_highways "Interstate highways") and a spur converge in Mobile. [Interstate 10](/wiki/Interstate_10 "Interstate 10") runs northeast to southwest across the city, while [Interstate 65](/wiki/Interstate_65 "Interstate 65") starts in Mobile at Interstate 10 and runs north. [Interstate 165](/wiki/Interstate_165_(Alabama) "Interstate 165 (Alabama)") connects to Interstate 65 north of the city in [Prichard](/wiki/Prichard,_Alabama "Prichard, Alabama") and joins Interstate 10 in downtown Mobile.[[263]](#cite_note-roads1-265) Mobile is well served by many major highway systems. US Highways [US 31](/wiki/U.S._Route_31_in_Alabama "U.S. Route 31 in Alabama"), [US 43](/wiki/US_43_(AL) "US 43 (AL)"), [US 45](/wiki/US_45_(AL) "US 45 (AL)"), [US 90](/wiki/US_90_(AL) "US 90 (AL)"), and [US 98](/wiki/US_98_(AL) "US 98 (AL)") radiate from Mobile traveling east, west, and north. Mobile has three routes east across the Mobile River and Mobile Bay into neighboring [Baldwin County](/wiki/Baldwin_County,_Alabama "Baldwin County, Alabama"). Interstate 10 leaves downtown through the [George Wallace Tunnel](/wiki/George_Wallace_Tunnel "George Wallace Tunnel") under the river and then over the bay across the [Jubilee Parkway](/wiki/Jubilee_Parkway "Jubilee Parkway") to [Spanish Fort](/wiki/Spanish_Fort,_Alabama "Spanish Fort, Alabama") and [Daphne](/wiki/Daphne,_Alabama "Daphne, Alabama"). US 98 leaves downtown through the [Bankhead Tunnel](/wiki/Bankhead_Tunnel "Bankhead Tunnel") under the river, onto [Blakeley Island](/wiki/Blakeley_Island_(Alabama) "Blakeley Island (Alabama)"), and then over the bay across the [Battleship Parkway](/wiki/Battleship_Parkway "Battleship Parkway") into Spanish Fort. US 90 travels over the [Cochrane–Africatown USA Bridge](/wiki/Cochrane%E2%80%93Africatown_USA_Bridge "Cochrane–Africatown USA Bridge") to the north of downtown onto Blakeley Island, where it becomes co-routed with US 98.[[263]](#cite_note-roads1-265)

Mobile's public transportation is the [Wave Transit System](/wiki/The_Wave_Transit_System "The Wave Transit System") which features buses with 18 fixed routes and neighborhood service.[[264]](#cite_note-buses-266) Baylinc is a public transportation bus service provided by the Baldwin Rural Transit System in cooperation with the Wave Transit System that provides service between eastern [Baldwin County](/wiki/Baldwin_County,_Alabama "Baldwin County, Alabama") and downtown Mobile. Baylinc operates Monday through Friday.[[265]](#cite_note-baylinc-267) [Greyhound Lines](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines") provides intercity bus service between Mobile and many locations throughout the United States. Mobile is served by several taxi and limousine services.[[266]](#cite_note-guide1-268)

To leverage Mobile's waterways for recreational use, The Three Mile Creek Greenway Trail is being designed and implemented under the instruction of the City Council. The linear park will ultimately span seven miles, from [Langan (Municipal) Park](/wiki/Langan_(Municipal)_Park "Langan (Municipal) Park") to Dr. Martin Luther King Junior Avenue, and include trailheads, sidewalks, and bike lanes. The existing greenway is centered at Tricentennial Park.[[267]](#cite_note-269)

[The Wave Transit System](/wiki/The_Wave_Transit_System "The Wave Transit System") provides fixed-route bus and [demand-response](/wiki/Demand-responsive_transit "Demand-responsive transit") service in Mobile.[[268]](#cite_note-270)

#### Water

The [Port of Mobile](/wiki/Port_of_Mobile "Port of Mobile") has public deepwater terminals with direct access to 1,500 miles (2,400 km) of inland and intracoastal waterways serving the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes"), the [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River") and [Tennessee river](/wiki/Tennessee_River "Tennessee River") valleys (via the [Tennessee-Tombigbee Waterway](/wiki/Tennessee-Tombigbee_Waterway "Tennessee-Tombigbee Waterway")), and the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico").[[253]](#cite_note-mobilechamber-255) The Alabama State Port Authority owns and operates the public terminals at the Port of Mobile.[[253]](#cite_note-mobilechamber-255) The public terminals handle [containerized](/wiki/Containerization "Containerization"), bulk, breakbulk, [roll-on/roll-off](/wiki/Roll-on/roll-off "Roll-on/roll-off"), and heavy-lift cargoes.[[253]](#cite_note-mobilechamber-255) The port is also home to private bulk terminal operators, as well as a number of highly specialized shipbuilding and repair companies with two of the largest floating dry docks on the Gulf Coast.[[253]](#cite_note-mobilechamber-255)

The city was a home port for [cruise ships](/wiki/Cruise_ship "Cruise ship") from [Carnival Cruise Lines](/wiki/Carnival_Cruise_Lines "Carnival Cruise Lines").[[269]](#cite_note-271) The first cruise ship to call the port home was the *[Holiday](/wiki/Holiday_(ship) "Holiday (ship)")*, which left the city in November 2009 so that a larger and newer ship could take its place. The *[Carnival Fantasy](/wiki/Carnival_Fantasy "Carnival Fantasy")* operated from Mobile from then on until the *[Carnival Elation](/wiki/Carnival_Elation "Carnival Elation")* arrived in May 2010.[[270]](#cite_note-272) In early 2011, Carnival announced that despite fully booked cruises, the company would cease operations from Mobile in October 2011. This cessation of cruise service left the city with an annual [debt service](/wiki/Interest "Interest") of around two million dollars related to the terminal.[[271]](#cite_note-273) In September 2015, Carnival announced that the *[Carnival Fantasy](/wiki/Carnival_Fantasy "Carnival Fantasy")* was relocating from Miami, Florida, to Mobile and would offer four- and five-night cruises to Mexico that started in November 2016 through November 2017.[[272]](#cite_note-274) Her first departure from Mobile left on November 9, 2016, on a five-night cruise to Cozumel and Progreso. *[Carnival Fascination](/wiki/Carnival_Fascination "Carnival Fascination")* will be replacing *Carnival Fantasy* in 2022.[[273]](#cite_note-275)<

### Utilities

[![Large L-shaped lake miles West of Mobile as seen from space](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Big_Creek_Lake.jpg/250px-Big_Creek_Lake.jpg)](/wiki/File:Big_Creek_Lake.jpg)

Big Creek Lake (left) and Mobile Bay (right) from space

Natural gas has been used in Mobile since 1836 when natural gas was used for lighting. Between 1933 and 2016 natural gas was provided by [Mobile Gas](/wiki/Mobile_Gas "Mobile Gas").[[274]](#cite_note-276) In 2016, [Spire Inc.](/wiki/Spire_Inc. "Spire Inc.") bought EnergySouth, Inc, the parent company of Mobile Gas and has been provide the service to the surrounding community since then.[[275]](#cite_note-city_utilities-277)[[276]](#cite_note-278)

The water for Mobile is provided by Mobile Area Water and Sewer System (MAWSS). When MAWSS was founded in 1814, it used Three-Mile Creek to provide water to the city.[[277]](#cite_note-279) In 1952, MAWSS constructed a dam at Tanner Williams road to block Big Creek stream creating [Big Creek Lake](/wiki/Big_Creek_Lake "Big Creek Lake"), a water reservoir for the city and the surrounding area.[[278]](#cite_note-280)

In 1884 with the demonstration of an incandescent lightbulb, Mobile Electric Company began providing electricity to the city as an alternative lighting method. In 1925, [Alabama Power](/wiki/Alabama_Power "Alabama Power") bought the Mobile Electric Company.[[279]](#cite_note-281) Electricity is still available from Alabama Power to this day.[[275]](#cite_note-city_utilities-277) Alabama Power owns and operates [James M. Barry Electric Generating Plant](/wiki/James_M._Barry_Electric_Generating_Plant "James M. Barry Electric Generating Plant"), fueled by coal and natural gas, and Theodore Cogen Facility, fueled by natural gas. These two power plants generate a total capacity of 3,519,870 kW for the surrounding communities.[[280]](#cite_note-282)

### Healthcare

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Mobile_Infirmary_front_facade.jpg/250px-Mobile_Infirmary_front_facade.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mobile_Infirmary_front_facade.jpg)

Mobile Infirmary Medical Center

Mobile serves the central Gulf Coast as a regional center for medicine.[[281]](#cite_note-283) The 200-year-old Mobile County Health Department (MCHD) provides education and preventive health services to Mobile and surrounding areas.[[282]](#cite_note-MCHD-284) Infirmary Health is Alabama's largest nonprofit, non-governmental health care system. It includes their flagship hospital Mobile Infirmary, two emergency centers and three outpatient surgery centers. Mobile Infirmary is the largest nonprofit hospital in the state.[[283]](#cite_note-Burnett-2023-285)[[284]](#cite_note-trauma-286)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Providence_Hospital_Mobile_01.jpg/250px-Providence_Hospital_Mobile_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Providence_Hospital_Mobile_01.jpg)

USA Health Providence Hospital

University of South Alabama Health (USA Health) operates 30 locations in the Mobile area, including three hospitals: [USA Health Providence Hospital](/wiki/USA_Health_Providence_Hospital "USA Health Providence Hospital"), the Children's & Women's Hospital, and University Hospital.[[283]](#cite_note-Burnett-2023-285) Providence Hospital was founded in 1854 by the [Daughters of Charity](/wiki/Daughters_of_Charity_of_Saint_Vincent_de_Paul "Daughters of Charity of Saint Vincent de Paul").[[284]](#cite_note-trauma-286) In 2023, USA Health acquired Providence Hospital from its private Catholic ownership.[[285]](#cite_note-Short-2025-287) The University of South Alabama Medical Center was founded in 1830 as the old city-owned [Mobile City Hospital](/wiki/Mobile_City_Hospital "Mobile City Hospital") and associated medical school. A teaching hospital, it is designated as Mobile's only [level I trauma center](/wiki/Level_I_trauma_center "Level I trauma center") by the [Alabama Department of Public Health](/wiki/Alabama_Department_of_Public_Health "Alabama Department of Public Health")[[284]](#cite_note-trauma-286)[[286]](#cite_note-usatrauma-288)[[287]](#cite_note-Alabama_Trauma_Centers-289) and is also a regional burn center.[[288]](#cite_note-mobchamber3-290) Children's & Women's Hospital is dedicated exclusively to the care of women and minors.[[288]](#cite_note-mobchamber3-290) In 2008, the University of South Alabama opened the Mitchell Cancer Center Institute, which includes the first academic cancer research center in the central Gulf Coast region.[[289]](#cite_note-291)

BayPointe Hospital and Children's Residential Services is the city's only psychiatric hospital. It houses a residential unit for children, an acute unit for children and adolescents, and an age-segregated involuntary hospital unit for adults undergoing evaluation ordered by the Mobile Probate Court.[[290]](#cite_note-baypointe-292) Springhill Medical Center was founded in 1975 and is Mobile's only for-profit facility.[[288]](#cite_note-mobchamber3-290)
